with Bidiagonal","user":{"login":"danielwe","id":5809017,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjU4MDkwMTc=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/5809017?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe","html_url":"https://github.com/danielwe","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"closed","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":2,"created_at":"2020-11-27T01:24:29Z","updated_at":"2024-11-26T11:38:13Z","closed_at":"2022-01-30T01:15:36Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","sub_issues_summary":{"total":0,"completed":0,"percent_completed":0},"active_lock_reason":null,"body":"This line allocates an unnecessary zero vector when doing matrix multiplication with a `Bidiagonal`:

https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/blob/377aa809eba31edbf9d73d22b85e801cd4664151/stdlib/LinearAlgebra/src/bidiag.jl#L431

Example:

```julia
using LinearAlgebra
using BenchmarkTools

N = 10
A = rand(N, N)
B = Bidiagonal(rand(N), rand(N - 1), :U)
C = Array{eltype(A)}(undef, N, N)

@btime mul!($C, $A, $B)

# Output:
  139.628 ns (1 allocation: 160 bytes)
```

The allocation could be eliminated without splitting up the combined bidiagonal/tridiagonal multiplication routines by instead returning a dummy zero vector that works something like this:

```julia
struct ZeroVector{T} end

Base.getindex(::ZeroVector{T}, ::Integer) where {T} = zero(T)
```

Is this an acceptable solution?","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/LinearAlgebra.jl/issues/794/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/LinearAlgebra.jl/issues/794/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":"completed"}}

functionality"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":{"login":"vtjnash","id":330950,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjMzMDk1MA==","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/330950?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/vtjnash","html_url":"https://github.com/vtjnash","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/vtjnash/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/vtjnash/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/vtjnash/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/vtjnash/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/vtjnash/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/vtjnash/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/vtjnash/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/vtjnash/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/vtjnash/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"assignees":[{"login":"vtjnash","id":330950,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjMzMDk1MA==","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/330950?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/vtjnash","html_url":"https://github.com/vtjnash","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/vtjnash/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/vtjnash/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/vtjnash/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/vtjnash/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/vtjnash/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/vtjnash/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/vtjnash/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/vtjnash/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/vtjnash/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false}],"milestone":null,"comments":6,"created_at":"2023-07-23T13:36:04Z","updated_at":"2024-10-17T20:07:58Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"It seems that the `sleep` function behavior when used in tasks running on different threads is heavily impacted by work taking place on the main thread.

Given the documentation of `sleep` function you would expect that `@time sleep(1)` will result in approximately `1-second` being reported by the `@time` evaluator. In the same way, you would expect that the following will take about 10-seconds in total:
```julia
@time for _ in 1:10
    sleep(1)
end

# Usually + MWE control (task1): 10 seconds +/- small value
# MWE context (current issue): 16+ seconds (can be altered by the period main thread is busy)
```

However, the above expectation is violated (**in a manner not explainable by the expected/usual variability** **in the** `sleep` and/or `@time` **behavior**) when `sleep` is called from tasks running on different threads and any of the following two conditions are met:

1. there is some work performed by the main thread, or `Libc.systemsleep(n)` is called on the main thread (showcased in my MWE)
2. the main thread is _free_, but many (~1000) spawned tasks call `sleep` concurrently (increasing the number of available threads results in worse results).

To give a clear example:
```julia
function sleeper(s, c, id)
    @info "sleeper $id executing on thread $(threadid()) "
    counter = 0
    @time "sleeper $id" while counter < c
        sleep(s)
        counter += 1
    end
end

@spawn sleeper(1, 10, 1)
```
Output like this would look normal:
```
sleeper 1: 10.021883 seconds (53.10 k allocations: 3.561 MiB)
```

However, the function `sleeper(1, 10, 1)` running in tasks spawned on different threads than main, can output values like the one below (when main thread is busy):
```
sleeper 1: 16.963197 seconds (13.87 k allocations: 957.833 KiB, 0.13% compilation time)
```

Or if you make the main really busy for a longer period of time:
```
sleeper 1: 29.944492 seconds (13.87 k allocations: 957.802 KiB, 0.06% compilation time)
```
And this is not about some weird print to `stdout` competition between tasks (we also have a `control` function, `realworker`, not using `sleep` to confirm my statement).

All this might make more sense if you run the MWE provided below.

Important note: the current issue is to be interpreted in the context of Julia being started with multiple threads. For consistency, let's assume `julia -t 6,3 script.jl`.

### Main MWE follows:

```julia
using Base.Threads, Dates

@info "started with $(nthreads()) threads"
@info "interactive threadpool: $(nthreads(:interactive))"
@info "default threadpool: $(nthreads(:default))"
println(repeat("*", 40))

function mainspin(s, c; withyield=false)
    @info "mainspin executing on thread $(threadid()) "
    ms = Millisecond(s * 1000)
    r = 0.0
    counter = 0
    while counter < c
        t = now()
        while (now() - t) < ms
            r += rand()
        end
        counter += 1
        if withyield
            yield()
        end

    end
end

function sleeper(s, c, id)
    @info "sleeper $id executing on thread $(threadid()) "
    counter = 0
    @time "sleeper $id" while counter < c
        sleep(s)
        counter += 1
    end
end

function realworker(s, c, id)
    @info "worker $id executing on thread $(threadid()) "
    ms = Millisecond(s * 1000)
    r = 0.0
    counter = 0
    @time "worker $id" while counter < c
        t = now()
        while (now() - t) < ms
            r += rand()
        end
        counter += 1
        yield()
    end
end

begin
    # phase 1
    @info "phase 1: control run - no main thread work"

    # yields 10 times (total time ~10 seconds)
    task1 = @spawn realworker(1.02, 10, 1) # 1.02 because weird @time printing overlap
    # yields 10 times (total time ~10 seconds)
    task11 = @spawn sleeper(1, 10, 1)

    wait(task1)
    wait(task11)

    # we waited for task1 and task11 to finish

    # phase 2
    println(repeat("*", 40))
    @info "phase 2: main thread work without yield"

    # yields 10 times (total time ~10 seconds)
    task2 = @spawn realworker(1, 10, 2)

    # yields 10 times (total time ~10 seconds)
    task22 = @spawn sleeper(1, 10, 2)

    # we wait 5 seconds before starting to spin
    # the main thread
    sleep(5)

    # now we start spinning 
    # while task2 and task22 are already running
    mainspin(1, 7)


    # main thread work does not impact the real worker
    # total time ~10 seconds
    wait(task2)

    # main thread work heavily impacting the sleeper
    # total time >15 seconds
    wait(task22)

    # phase 3
    println(repeat("*", 40))
    @info "phase 3: main thread work with yield"

    # yields 10 times (total time ~10 seconds)
    task3 = @spawn realworker(1, 10, 3)

    # yields 10 times (total time ~10 seconds)
    task33 = @spawn sleeper(1, 10, 3)

    # we wait 5 seconds before starting to spin
    # the main thread
    sleep(5)

    # now we start spinning with yield
    # while task3 and task33 are running
    mainspin(1, 7, withyield=true)


    # main thread work does not impact 
    # the real worker - total time ~10 seconds
    wait(task3)

    # main thread work (with yield) still impacting the sleeper
    # but we consistently get better times (total time ~13 seconds)
    wait(task33)
end
```

Output with `julia -t 6,3 script.jl`:
```
[ Info: started with 6 threads
[ Info: interactive threadpool: 3
[ Info: default threadpool: 6
****************************************
[ Info: phase 1: control run - no main thread work
[ Info: worker 1 executing on thread 4 
[ Info: sleeper 1 executing on thread 8 
sleeper 1: 10.020176 seconds (51 allocations: 1.438 KiB)
worker 1: 10.199500 seconds (53.62 k allocations: 3.704 MiB, 0.36% compilation time)
****************************************
[ Info: phase 2: main thread work without yield
[ Info: worker 2 executing on thread 7 
[ Info: sleeper 2 executing on thread 5 
[ Info: mainspin executing on thread 1 
worker 2: 9.999656 seconds (47.22 k allocations: 3.243 MiB, 0.24% compilation time)
sleeper 2: 16.974156 seconds (13.87 k allocations: 957.802 KiB, 0.14% compilation time)
****************************************
[ Info: phase 3: main thread work with yield
[ Info: worker 3 executing on thread 9 
[ Info: sleeper 3 executing on thread 5 
[ Info: mainspin executing on thread 1 
worker 3: 9.999733 seconds (37.05 k allocations: 2.618 MiB, 0.23% compilation time)
sleeper 3: 13.979589 seconds (2.67 k allocations: 249.790 KiB, 0.16% compilation time)
```

It would be speculative on my part to derive hard conclusions about the root cause of this issue. There is a discourse topic that I open and some users are proposing some hypotheses - [feel free to check it out](https://discourse.julialang.org/t/bug-in-sleep-function-main-thread-work-affecting-sleep-duration-on-running-tasks/101911?u=algunion). 

The underlying cause of the above issue seems to extend to various packages (for example, non-blocking listen from `HTTP.jl` responsivity is highly dependent on the main threat not doing work - and that is not caused by the direct `sleep` usage). Essential to mention that `HTTP.serve!/HTTP.listen!` is spawned to the `:interactive` threadpool.

#### Libc.systemsleep MWE version
<details>
<summary>The `Libc.systemsleep` version of the above MWE</summary>

```julia
using Base.Threads, Dates

@info "started with $(nthreads()) threads"
@info "interactive threadpool: $(nthreads(:interactive))"
@info "default threadpool: $(nthreads(:default))"
println(repeat("*", 40))

function mainspin(s, c; withyield=false)
    @info "mainspin executing on thread $(threadid()) "
    r = 0.0
    counter = 0
    while counter < c
        Libc.systemsleep(s)
        counter += 1
        if withyield
            yield()
        end
    end
end

function sleeper(s, c, id)
    @info "sleeper $id executing on thread $(threadid()) "
    counter = 0
    @time "sleeper $id" while counter < c
        sleep(s)
        counter += 1
    end
end

function libcworker(s, c, id)
    @info "worker $id executing on thread $(threadid()) "
    r = 0.0
    counter = 0
    @time "worker $id" while counter < c
        Libc.systemsleep(s)
        counter += 1
        yield()
    end
end

begin
    # phase 1
    @info "phase 1: control run - no main thread work"

    # yields 10 times (total time ~10 seconds)
    task1 = @spawn libcworker(1.02, 10, 1) # 1.02 because weird @time printing overlap
    # yields 10 times (total time ~10 seconds)
    task11 = @spawn sleeper(1, 10, 1)

    wait(task1)
    wait(task11)

    # we waited for task1 and task11 to finish

    # phase 2
    println(repeat("*", 40))
    @info "phase 2: main thread work without yield"

    # yields 10 times (total time ~10 seconds)
    task2 = @spawn libcworker(1, 10, 2)

    # yields 10 times (total time ~10 seconds)
    task22 = @spawn sleeper(1, 10, 2)

    # we wait 5 seconds before starting to spin
    # the main thread
    sleep(5)

    # now we start spinning 
    # while task2 and task22 are already running
    mainspin(1, 7)


    # main thread work does not impact the real worker
    # total time ~10 seconds
    wait(task2)

    # main thread work heavily impacting the sleeper
    # total time >15 seconds
    wait(task22)

    # phase 3
    println(repeat("*", 40))
    @info "phase 3: main thread work with yield"

    # yields 10 times (total time ~10 seconds)
    task3 = @spawn libcworker(1, 10, 3)

    # yields 10 times (total time ~10 seconds)
    task33 = @spawn sleeper(1, 10, 3)

    # we wait 5 seconds before starting to spin
    # the main thread
    sleep(5)

    # now we start spinning with yield
    # while task3 and task33 are running
    mainspin(1, 7, withyield=true)


    # main thread work does not impact 
    # the real worker - total time ~10 seconds
    wait(task3)

    # main thread work (with yield) still impacting the sleeper
    # but we consistently get better times (total time ~13 seconds)
    wait(task33)
end
```

{"action":"opened","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/56221","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/56221/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/56221/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/56221/events","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/56221","id":2596095781,"node_id":"I_kwDOABkWpM6avUcl","number":56221,"title":"Vararg can follow optional arg if declared with ellipsis but not with `Vararg`","user":{"login":"danielwe","id":5809017,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjU4MDkwMTc=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/5809017?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe","html_url":"https://github.com/danielwe","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":0,"created_at":"2024-10-18T00:47:20Z","updated_at":"2024-10-18T00:47:20Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"```julia-repl
julia> f(x=1, args...) = (x, args...)  # works as expected
f (generic function with 2 methods)

julia> g(x=1, args::Vararg) = (x, args...)
ERROR: syntax: optional positional arguments must occur at end around REPL[17]:1
Stacktrace:
 [1] top-level scope
   @ REPL[17]:1
```

It would be great if the latter form worked, as `Vararg` allows more flexible type constraints and specializations than `...`, including working around avoided specialization as per https://docs.julialang.org/en/v1/manual/performance-tips/#Be-aware-of-when-Julia-avoids-specializing","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/56221/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/56221/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null}}

{"action":"opened","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/AllocCheck.jl/issues/81","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/AllocCheck.jl","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/AllocCheck.jl/issues/81/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/AllocCheck.jl/issues/81/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/AllocCheck.jl/issues/81/events","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/AllocCheck.jl/issues/81","id":2451734249,"node_id":"I_kwDOKTlkfc6SIn7p","number":81,"title":"`multiple call sites` obscures allocation origin","user":{"login":"danielwe","id":5809017,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjU4MDkwMTc=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/5809017?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe","html_url":"https://github.com/danielwe","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":0,"created_at":"2024-08-06T21:38:25Z","updated_at":"2024-08-06T21:38:25Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"Allocations reported by AllocCheck often have stack traces that only contain stack frames from Julia core/Base (array/dict/set constructors, locks, gc code, etc.) before terminating with `multiple call sites`. This is unhelpful for debugging and makes it hard to filter out allocations that a developer might consider benign.

Could these cases instead be split into multiple allocations, one for each origin, each with a stack trace going back to the top-level call?

As an example, see the code below, where a simple log statement results in 95 reported allocations, and an attempt to filter out the logging-related ones only removes 30 of them.

```julia-repl
julia> using AllocCheck

julia> f() = @warn "Watch out!"
f (generic function with 1 method)

julia> allocs = check_allocs(f, ());

julia> length(allocs)
95

julia> allocs_not_from_loggingJL = filter(allocs) do alloc
           for frame in alloc.backtrace
               (frame.file == Symbol("./logging.jl")) && return false
           end
           return true
       end;

julia> length(allocs_not_from_loggingJL)
65

julia> allocs_not_from_loggingJL[1]  # example of stack trace terminating with "multiple call sites"
Allocation of String in ./strings/string.jl:90
  | @eval _string_n(n::Integer) = $(Expr(:foreigncall, QuoteNode(:jl_alloc_string), Ref{String}, Expr(:call, Expr(:core, :svec), :Csize_t), 1, QuoteNode((:ccall,0xe)), :(convert(Csize_t, n))))

Stacktrace:
 [1] _string_n
   @ ./strings/string.jl:90 [inlined]
 [2] StringVector
   @ ./iobuffer.jl:32 [inlined]
 [3] _similar_data
   @ ./iobuffer.jl:142 [inlined]
 [4] ensureroom
   @ ./iobuffer.jl:337 [inlined]
 [5] unsafe_write(to::IOBuffer, p::Ptr{UInt8}, nb::UInt64)
   @ Base ./iobuffer.jl:458
 [6] multiple call sites
   @ unknown:0
```","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/AllocCheck.jl/issues/81/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/AllocCheck.jl/issues/81/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null}}

{"action":"opened","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/Pkg.jl/issues/4006","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/Pkg.jl","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/Pkg.jl/issues/4006/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/Pkg.jl/issues/4006/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/Pkg.jl/issues/4006/events","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/Pkg.jl/issues/4006","id":2490296878,"node_id":"I_kwDOBOhtSc6Ubuou","number":4006,"title":"`ResolverError` coloring should be decided in `showerror`, not on construction","user":{"login":"danielwe","id":5809017,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjU4MDkwMTc=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/5809017?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe","html_url":"https://github.com/danielwe","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":0,"created_at":"2024-08-27T20:37:05Z","updated_at":"2024-08-27T20:37:05Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"A `ResolverError` has color hardcoded in the message if `stderr` supports color, as the message is formatted on construction using the following function:

https://github.com/JuliaLang/Pkg.jl/blob/8b2c0f32939042e5d77da05d2825de6ec56487e9/src/Resolve/graphtype.jl#L571-L576

This is unfortunate when the error message isn't actually printed to `stderr`, for example in Pluto:

![image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/ba132ea4-7072-4a45-a84e-22bd047e4b02)

Perhaps it would be better to store data rather than a preformatted message in the `ResolverError` object, and only format the message in the `showerror(io, ::ResolverError)` method, with or without color depending on `io`.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/Pkg.jl/issues/4006/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/Pkg.jl/issues/4006/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null}}

{"action":"opened","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/53505","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/53505/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/53505/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/53505/events","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/53505","id":2158259874,"node_id":"I_kwDOABkWpM6ApG6i","number":53505,"title":"Inconsistent int-float promotion in `hypot`","user":{"login":"danielwe","id":5809017,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjU4MDkwMTc=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/5809017?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe","html_url":"https://github.com/danielwe","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":0,"created_at":"2024-02-28T07:03:54Z","updated_at":"2024-02-28T07:03:54Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"`hypot(::Int64, ::Float32)` promotes the arguments and result to `Float64`, contrary to the behavior of `promote`, which gives `Float32` in this case.

```julia-repl
julia> hypot(1, 3.0f0)
3.1622776601683795

julia> promote(1, 3.0f0)
(1.0f0, 3.0f0)
```

The culprits are the following method definitions:

https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/blob/71f68b4ce9189e64f320631f3f74ffb3dd10e875/base/math.jl#L798-L799

Seems like this could be rewritten as follows for consistency with `promote`:

```julia
hypot(x::Number, y::Number) = _hypot(float.(promote(x, y))...)
hypot(x::Number, y::Number, xs::Number...) = _hypot(float.(promote(x, y, xs...)) 
```","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/53505/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/53505/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null}}

 7. Avoid naming a package closely to an existing package
      * `Websocket` is too close to `WebSockets` and can be confusing to users. Rather use a new name such as `SimpleWebsockets`.
 
+8. Avoid using a distinctive name that is already in use in a well known, unrelated project.
+     * Don't use the names `Tkinter.jl`, `TkinterGUI.jl`, etc. for a package that is unrelated
+       to the popular `tkinter` python package. Users are likely to assume a connection that
+       does not exist.
+     * It's okay to name a package `HTTP.jl` even though it is unrelated to the popular rust

algebra"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":7,"created_at":"2023-04-27T19:56:50Z","updated_at":"2023-12-21T15:13:13Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"draft":false,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/pulls/49546","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/49546","diff_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/49546.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/49546.patch","merged_at":null},"body":"## Background: 
We have a type `SymTridiagonal` which is an ~unnatural abomination~ type representing *symmetric* tridiagonal matrices, but we have no such type for *hermitian* tridiagonal matrices which are much more natural objects in linear algebra. 

This leads to an unfortunate situation where trying to get the eigenvalues of a real `SymTridiagonal` matrix is very fast, but if you have complex elements and a hermitian structure, then you end up hitting the generic fallback and performance will greatly suffer. 

The upside is that for any hermitian tridiagonal matrix `A`, there exists a diagonal unitary matrix `S` such that `S' * A * S` is a real symmetric tridiagonal matrix. `B = S' * A * S` will have the same main diagonal as `A`, and the off diagonals will simply be the absolute values of the off-diagonals of `A`. 

This is something known by experts, an sounds plausible to people with some experience, but I think isn't common particularly common knowledge amongst wider users of linear algebra, so if there's an easy way we can do this for them automatically, that'd be good. 

## This PR

I wasn't really sure what would be the best approach would be to put this transformation into LinearAlgebra, so I figured I'd just do the most obvious thing, put it out there and then request advice for how to integrate it better into the system.

Ideally, this would work with regular `Tridiagonal{<:Complex}` matrices by just checking if they're hermitian, but I was a bit wary of the resulting dispatch gymnastics, so I opted to only operate on `Hermitian{Complex{T}, <:Tridiagonal}` for now.

The approach is that for `eigen`, I generate the similarity transform `S`, and the  then calculate the eigenvalues and eigenvectors of 
```julia
B = SymTridiagonal(real.(A.d), abs.(A.dl))
```
and the plain eigenvalues, as well as the eigenvectors which are transformed by `S`. 

For `eigvals`, that transformation is not necessary, so I simply do `eigvals(SymTridiagonal(real.(d), abs.(dl)); kwargs...)` which saves us a matmul relative to `eigen`

Any advice or comments on the implementation / approach before I start writing up some tests and such? 

## Performance comparisons
Before:
```julia
#+begin_src julia
let N = 50
    D = rand(N) |> complex
    E = rand(ComplexF64, N-1)

    A = Tridiagonal(E, D, adjoint.(E))
    @btime eigvals(Hermitian($A))
    @btime eigen(Hermitian($A))
end;
#+end_src

#+RESULTS:
:   106.560 μs (13 allocations: 80.59 KiB)
:   265.460 μs (15 allocations: 158.69 KiB)
```

and after
```julia
#+begin_src julia
let N = 50
    D = rand(N) |> complex
    E = rand(ComplexF64, N-1)

    A = Tridiagonal(E, D, adjoint.(E))
    @btime eigvals(Hermitian($A))
    @btime eigen(Hermitian($A))
end;
#+end_src

#+RESULTS:
:   34.720 μs (6 allocations: 2.78 KiB)
:   110.910 μs (35 allocations: 74.98 KiB)
```

_________________________

**Edit:**  I forgot to mention in the original post, this algorithm was sketched out in https://web.archive.org/web/20201028202318/http://icl.cs.utk.edu/lapack-forum/viewtopic.php?f=2&t=3592# , and it was brought to my attention by @stevengj  with this Discourse post: this warrant a new `HermTridiagonal` type? That might be the first LinearAlgebra structured matrix type that does not represent a LAPACK-supported structure, but with the resulting symmetry between `Symmetric/Hermitian` and `SymTridiagonal/HermTridiagonal`, it might be the best non-breaking way forward.

Related reading: #43983.

> There doesn't seem to be such a direct hermitian routine

As far as I understand, LAPACK's support for real symmetric tridiagonal matrices exists not so much for the interest in such matrices in their own right, but because they are an intermediate representation in the eigendecomposition of hermitian matrices, much like Hessenberg matrices for QR decomposition. Thus `SymTridiagonal` exists and is `Sym` rather than `Herm` because LinearAlgebra is in large part designed as a LAPACK wrapper. Somewhat tangential, but @andreasnoack's comment here gives an idea about why these types exist and work the way they do: https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/30137#issuecomment-441800287

> The way I think about Bidiagonal, SymTridiagonal, and Tridiagional is that they are the building blocks for SVD, the symmetric eigenproblem, and a special case of LU respectively and that it shouldn't really be expected that all linear algebra methods work for these.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1866458178/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

We have a type `SymTridiagonal` which is an ~unnatural abomination~ type representing *symmetric* tridiagonal matrices, but we have no such type for *hermitian* tridiagonal matrices which are much more natural objects in linear algebra. 

This leads to an unfortunate situation where trying to get the eigenvalues of a real `SymTridiagonal` matrix is very fast, but if you have complex elements and a hermitian structure, then you end up hitting the generic fallback and performance will greatly suffer. 

The upside is that for any hermitian tridiagonal matrix `A`, there exists a diagonal unitary matrix `S` such that `S' * A * S` is a real symmetric tridiagonal matrix. `B = S' * A * S` will have the same main diagonal as `A`, and the off diagonals will simply be the absolute values of the off-diagonals of `A`. 

This is something known by experts, an sounds plausible to people with some experience, but I think isn't common particularly common knowledge amongst wider users of linear algebra, so if there's an easy way we can do this for them automatically, that'd be good. 

## This PR

I wasn't really sure what would be the best approach would be to put this transformation into LinearAlgebra, so I figured I'd just do the most obvious thing, put it out there and then request advice for how to integrate it better into the system.

Ideally, this would work with regular `Tridiagonal{<:Complex}` matrices by just checking if they're hermitian, but I was a bit wary of the resulting dispatch gymnastics, so I opted to only operate on `Hermitian{Complex{T}, <:Tridiagonal}` for now.

The approach is that for `eigen`, I generate the similarity transform `S`, and the  then calculate the eigenvalues and eigenvectors of 
```julia
B = SymTridiagonal(real.(A.d), abs.(A.dl))
```
and the plain eigenvalues, as well as the eigenvectors which are transformed by `S`. 

For `eigvals`, that transformation is not necessary, so I simply do `eigvals(SymTridiagonal(real.(d), abs.(dl)); kwargs...)` which saves us a matmul relative to `eigen`

Any advice or comments on the implementation / approach before I start writing up some tests and such? 

## Performance comparisons
Before:
```julia
#+begin_src julia
let N = 50
    D = rand(N) |> complex
    E = rand(ComplexF64, N-1)

    A = Tridiagonal(E, D, adjoint.(E))
    @btime eigvals(Hermitian($A))
    @btime eigen(Hermitian($A))
end;
#+end_src

#+RESULTS:
:   106.560 μs (13 allocations: 80.59 KiB)
:   265.460 μs (15 allocations: 158.69 KiB)
```

and after
```julia
#+begin_src julia
let N = 50
    D = rand(N) |> complex
    E = rand(ComplexF64, N-1)

    A = Tridiagonal(E, D, adjoint.(E))
    @btime eigvals(Hermitian($A))
    @btime eigen(Hermitian($A))
end;
#+end_src

#+RESULTS:
:   34.720 μs (6 allocations: 2.78 KiB)
:   110.910 μs (35 allocations: 74.98 KiB)
```

_________________________

I don't think it's really **that** unusual though to want to specialized on `Hermitian{<:Complex,<:Tridiagonal}`.

It's certainly not unusual to want to diagonalize a hermitian tridiagonal matrix, I've often wanted that myself. It's also reasonable that `Hermitian{<:Complex,<:Tridiagonal}` hits the optimized method. I'm just thinking about consistency in the zoo of structured matrix types and their behavior, the lack of which has always been a bit of a stumbling block with LinearAlgebra.jl.

Perhaps this PR should also add methods for `Hermitian{<:Real,<:Tridiagonal}` and `Symmetric{<:Real,<:Tridiagonal}`? Those would be simple forwarding methods like what you're doing for `eigvals`, except you don't even have to use `abs`. I think this would help a lot with consistency and reliability in dispatching to the best method available.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1868258955/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

This is still inaccurate. The first entry in the default `LOAD_PATH` is `"@"`, and according to the documentation,

> `"@"` refers to the "current active environment", the initial value of which is initially determined by the [`JULIA_PROJECT`](https://docs.julialang.org/en/v1/manual/environment-variables/#JULIA_PROJECT) environment variable or the `--project` command-line option.

However, what happens when calling `activate` without arguments is that the project set through `JULIA_PROJECT` or `--project` is deliberately forgotten (by setting `Base.ACTIVE_PROJECT[] = nothing`). This turns `"@"` into a simple redirect to the next `LOAD_PATH` entry. In other words, `activate` without arguments isn't simply looking at `LOAD_PATH`, it's changing the meaning of the entries in `LOAD_PATH`.

How about:

> If no argument is given to `activate`, then discard any active project set via the [`JULIA_PROJECT`](https://docs.julialang.org/en/v1/manual/environment-variables/#JULIA_PROJECT) environment variable, the `--project` command-line option, or any previous calls to `activate`. Unless `LOAD_PATH` has been modified, the result is to activate the default `@v#.#` environment.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/Pkg.jl/issues/comments/1676232090/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

Not the expert here, I had never looked at this code before I wrote the previous comment, but it looks like it makes a difference in `load_path_expand`: https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/blob/fd38d50cefd17c2e31df34c6b6c4a5b7c721c57d/base/initdefs.jl#L250

Without it, code loading won't know about the active project:

```julia
(@v1.9) pkg> activate --temp

julia> popfirst!(LOAD_PATH)
"@"

julia> using EnumX

home project: remove all uses of this (no longer does anything as of https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/36434)

* Previously there was no convenient way to activate the current project from the Pkg REPL or API. This changes `Pkg.activate()` so that if there is no active project to deactivate, it instead activates `Base.current_project()`. This means that we can now say that `julia --project` is exactly equivlent to `julia` followed by `pkg> activate`.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/Pkg.jl/issues/1891/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/Pkg.jl/issues/1891/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/Pkg.jl/issues/comments/1678416450","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/Pkg.jl/pull/1891#issuecomment-1678416450","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/Pkg.jl/issues/1891","id":1678416450,"node_id":"IC_kwDOBOhtSc5kCppC","user":{"login":"danielwe","id":5809017,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjU4MDkwMTc=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/5809017?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe","html_url":"https://github.com/danielwe","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2023-08-15T04:34:57Z","updated_at":"2023-08-15T04:34:57Z","author_association":"NONE","body":"Coming here from #3547 and just wanted to confirm that this is the intended behavior for repeated no-arg calls to `activate`:

* First call: discard and forget any active environment set via `JULIA_PROJECT` or the `--project` parameter at startup, or via subsequent `activate` calls with argument. Unless `LOAD_PATH` has been modified, this activates the base `@v1.x` environment.
* Second call: activate `current_project()`, which is determined based on the current working directory (as if `--project=@.` were passed at startup)
* Subsequent calls: toggle between the latter two

I confess I'd be surprised by this behavior, specifically having the environment I provided on the command line forgotten while an unrelated non-base environment is brought into orbit. Wouldn't it make more sense to toggle back to the previously active environment rather than `current_project()`?","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/Pkg.jl/issues/comments/1678416450/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

Yes, that's simple and clear. Though I do think it's worth pointing out what this implies in the usual case, i.e., that it activates `@v1.x`. You shouldn't have to read the docs for `LOAD_PATH` to understand what `activate` does when you haven't touched `LOAD_PATH`.



> (By the way, the comment line
> 
> > [`` # `@` in JULIA_LOAD_PATH is expanded early (at startup time) ``](https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/blob/fd38d50cefd17c2e31df34c6b6c4a5b7c721c57d/base/initdefs.jl#L250)
> 
> seems to be inaccurate?)

No, this appears to be correct:

```bash
user@host:~/tmp$ ls
Manifest.toml  Project.toml
user@host:~/tmp$ JULIA_LOAD_PATH="@." julia -q --startup-file=no
julia> LOAD_PATH
1-element Vector{String}:
 "/home/daniel/tmp/Project.toml"
```

I suppose you'd have to look at the code that processes environment variables at startup to see how this is implemented.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/Pkg.jl/issues/comments/1679502535/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

{"action":"opened","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/50298","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/50298/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/50298/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/50298/events","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/50298","id":1776092022,"node_id":"I_kwDOABkWpM5p3QN2","number":50298,"title":"`Channel` iteration uses inconsistent termination criteria","user":{"login":"danielwe","id":5809017,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjU4MDkwMTc=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/5809017?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe","html_url":"https://github.com/danielwe","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":0,"created_at":"2023-06-27T04:58:31Z","updated_at":"2023-06-27T04:58:31Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"Since #45966, `Channel` iteration uses inconsistent termination criteria. Consider the implementation:

https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/blob/269d3501a645b1a7807c1676092acd0c9719459d/base/channels.jl#L591-L605

In the absence of a race condition, iteration terminates when the channel is closed and empty as determined by the conditional. However, if you end up in the catch block, the criterion for deciding whether to terminate iteration (i.e., you're just here because of a race condition while closing the channel) or rethrow (there's an actual problem) is based on the type and value of the exception, not on the state of the channel.

The two criteria are consistent if the channel was closed using `close(c)`, but not if you attach a custom exception as in `close(c, CustomException())`. I can see three different ways to achieve consistency without reverting to the old, slower design.

* _Alternative 1:_ Deprecate `close(c::Channel, excp::Exception)` in favor of `close(c::Channel, msg::String)`, which sets `c.excp = InvalidStateException(msg, :closed)`. That way, `take!(c)` on a closed channel always raises an error that satisfies the termination criterion.

* _Alternative 2:_ Decide that closed channel iteration should always terminate normally, regardless of `c.excp`. To achieve this, relax the catch block criterion to only verify that the channel is closed and empty and that the exception is indeed `c.excp`:

```julia
function iterate(c::Channel, state=nothing)
    if isopen(c) || isready(c)
        try
            return (take!(c), nothing)
        catch e
            if !(isopen(c) || isready(c)) && (c.excp === e || (c.excp === nothing && isequal(e, closed_exception())))
                # c.excp === nothing included for consistency with check_channel_state(c)
                return nothing
            else
                rethrow()
            end
        end
    else
        return nothing
    end
end
```

* _Alternative 3:_ Stick to the behavior from before #45966, such that closed channel iteration only terminates normally if `take!(c)` would throw `InvalidStateException(msg, :closed)`. To enforce this, perform an extra inspection in the `else` block:

```julia
function iterate(c::Channel, state=nothing)
    if isopen(c) || isready(c)
        try
            return (take!(c), nothing)
        catch e
            if isa(e, InvalidStateException) && e.state === :closed
                return nothing
            else
                rethrow()
            end
        end
    else
        # adapted from check_channel_state(c); this is what take!(c) would do before throwing
        (@atomic :acquire c.state) === :open && concurrency_violation()
        e = c.excp
        # e === nothing included for consistency with check_channel_state(c)
        if (e === nothing) || (isa(e, InvalidStateException) && e.state === :closed)
            return nothing
        else
            throw(e)
        end
    end
end
```

Consider the implementation:

https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/blob/269d3501a645b1a7807c1676092acd0c9719459d/base/channels.jl#L591-L605

In the absence of a race condition, iteration terminates when the channel is closed and empty as determined by the conditional. However, if you end up in the catch block, the criterion for deciding whether to terminate iteration (i.e., you're just here because of a race condition while closing the channel) or rethrow (there's an actual problem) is based on the type and value of the exception, not on the state of the channel.

The two criteria are consistent if the channel was closed using `close(c)`, but not if you attach a custom exception as in `close(c, CustomException())`. I can see three different ways to achieve consistency without reverting to the old, slower design.

* _Alternative 1:_ Deprecate `close(c::Channel, excp::Exception)` in favor of `close(c::Channel, msg::String)`, which sets `c.excp = InvalidStateException(msg, :closed)`. That way, `take!(c)` on a closed channel always raises an error that satisfies the termination criterion.

* _Alternative 2:_ Decide that closed channel iteration should always terminate normally, regardless of `c.excp`. To achieve this, relax the catch block criterion to only verify that the channel is closed and empty and that the exception is indeed `c.excp`:

```julia
function iterate(c::Channel, state=nothing)
    if isopen(c) || isready(c)
        try
            return (take!(c), nothing)
        catch e
            if !(isopen(c) || isready(c)) && (c.excp === e || (c.excp === nothing && isequal(e, closed_exception())))
                # c.excp === nothing included for consistency with check_channel_state(c)
                return nothing
            else
                rethrow()
            end
        end
    else
        return nothing
    end
end
```

* _Alternative 3:_ Stick to the behavior from before #45966, such that closed channel iteration only terminates normally if `take!(c)` would throw `InvalidStateException(msg, :closed)`. To enforce this, perform an extra inspection in the `else` block:

```julia
function iterate(c::Channel, state=nothing)
    if isopen(c) || isready(c)
        try
            return (take!(c), nothing)
        catch e
            if isa(e, InvalidStateException) && e.state === :closed
                return nothing
            else
                rethrow()
            end
        end
    else
        # adapted from check_channel_state(c); this is what take!(c) would do before throwing
        (@atomic :acquire c.state) === :open && concurrency_violation()
        e = c.excp
        # e === nothing included for consistency with check_channel_state(c)
        if (e === nothing) || (isa(e, InvalidStateException) && e.state === :closed)
            return nothing
        else
            throw(e)
        end
    end
end
```

The point is that it's currently not deterministic whether this will throw or not. If the channel is only consumed by a single task, then it will never throw.  It will only throw if the channel is consumed by two or more concurrent tasks and you happen upon a race condition such that the channel is emptied between the check for `isopen(c) || isready(c)` and the actual call to `take!`. This non-deterministic behavior is the issue I'm raising.

Sounds like you support alternative 3. (Note that even in this case, if the channel is closed with a custom exception while non-empty, it will continue iterating normally until it's empty and _then_ throw.)","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1609995034/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

julia> using Distributed

julia> f = remotecall(error, 1)
Future(...)

julia> fetch(f)
RemoteException(...)
```

The `RemoteException` is returned, not thrown as claimed in the docs (https://docs.julialang.org/en/v1/stdlib/Distributed/#Base.fetch-Tuple{Distributed.Future}):

> ```julia
>     fetch(x::Future)
> ```
>

```julia

Status `~/myenv/Project.toml` (empty project)

julia> Pkg.activate(; temp=true)
  Activating new project at `/tmp/jl_gmivnG`

julia> Pkg.status()
Status `/tmp/jl_gmivnG/Project.toml` (empty project)

julia> Pkg.activate()
  Activating project at `~/.julia/environments/v1.8`

julia> Pkg.status()
Status `~/.julia/environments/v1.8/Project.toml`
  [...]
```

Clearly `Pkg.activate()` activates the default environment `@v#.#`, not the environment specified on the command line as claimed in the docs.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/Pkg.jl/issues/comments/1426264173/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

{"action":"opened","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/DifferentialEquations.jl/issues/901","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/DifferentialEquations.jl","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/DifferentialEquations.jl/issues/901/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/DifferentialEquations.jl/issues/901/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/DifferentialEquations.jl/issues/901/events","html_url":"https://github.com/SciML/DifferentialEquations.jl/issues/901","id":1381368476,"node_id":"I_kwDOA3ziS85SVgKc","number":901,"title":"Inconsistent choice of default algorithm order when eltype is complex","user":{"login":"danielwe","id":5809017,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjU4MDkwMTc=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/5809017?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe","html_url":"https://github.com/danielwe","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":0,"created_at":"2022-09-21T19:01:55Z","updated_at":"2022-09-21T19:01:55Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"The default choice of algorithm for ODEs tries to infer the desired level of precision by looking at number types: if it's not the "ordinary" Float32/64, it gives you a high-order algorithm because you're probably using BigFloat or some other exotic high-precision type. However, ComplexF32/64 will sometimes also land you in the high-order branch because it's not Float32/64. In particular, that happens here:

https://github.com/SciML/DifferentialEquations.jl/blob/8b4d07c5523eaf905bf245fbc439a2c820c1cdc3/src/ode_default_alg.jl#L68

However, it does not happen in the corresponding branch for huge (N > 10000), explicit-only problems:

https://github.com/SciML/DifferentialEquations.jl/blob/8b4d07c5523eaf905bf245fbc439a2c820c1cdc3/src/ode_default_alg.jl#L35

However, this has its own problems: _any_ complex type escapes the high-order branch, including Complex{BigFloat}---not just ComplexF32/F64, as was probably the intention.

Suggested fix: use `uRealtype = real(eltype(u0))` instead of `uEltype = eltype(u0)` in the heuristics.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/DifferentialEquations.jl/issues/901/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/DifferentialEquations.jl/issues/901/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null}}

functions"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":6,"created_at":"2022-04-07T08:00:53Z","updated_at":"2022-09-28T21:09:12Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"draft":false,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/pulls/44893","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/44893","diff_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/44893.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/44893.patch","merged_at":null},"body":"Hi all, I was going to raise an issue but figured I might as well frame it in terms of a proposed solution.

I stumbled upon a case where I couldn't `isapprox` two GPU arrays because they hit this `zip` trying to pull elements one by one. Using `mapreduce` instead should fix the problem for these and any other array types that don't like sequential iteration.

The drawback is that this hurts performance for regular CPU arrays (#38558). The slowdown is worse in cases that admit an early short-circuit:

```julia
julia> A = randn(100000); B = copy(A); A[end] = Inf;  # No short-circuit

julia> @btime isapprox(A, B);
  172.186 μs (2 allocations: 781.30 KiB)

julia> @btime isapprox_new(A, B)
  292.771 μs (12 allocations: 879.28 KiB)

julia> A[1] = 0;  # Immediate short-circuit possible

julia> @btime isapprox(A, B);
  79.641 μs (2 allocations: 781.30 KiB)

julia> @btime isapprox_new(A, B);
  292.528 μs (12 allocations: 879.28 KiB)
```

However, note that:

* This branch is a fallback for an exceptional case
* Much worse performance can occur for unrelated reasons, for example:

```julia
julia> A[1] = Inf;  # Why does this make norm(A - B) an order of magnitude slower??

julia> @btime isapprox(A, B);
  726.410 μs (2 allocations: 781.30 KiB)

julia> @btime isapprox_new(A, B);
  966.347 μs (12 allocations: 879.28 KiB)
```

So maybe a temporary slowdown (until `mapreduce` is improved) in this exceptional branch is an acceptable price to pay for more generic code? That way GPUArrays.jl won't have to duplicate the entire method just to change one line.

~Bonus: some `mapreduce` calls in `generic_norm` are really just straightforward applications of `minimum/maximum/sum`, so I rewrote them as such for improved clarity. The behavior should be unchanged. Since this change also concerns reductions in generic linear algebra routines I thought a single PR might suffice even if this is really a separate issue.~

**About tests**

* `isapprox` isn't explicitly tested on arrays. On the other hand, it's the foundation for almost every other test, so in that sense, it's very thoroughly tested.
* ~The `generic_norm` rewrites shouldn't change behavior, so existing tests should suffice.~

EDIT: rolled back the mapreduce simplifications in `norm` since they trigger #44906 and are unrelated to the main issue here.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/44893/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/44893/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1261467633","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/44893#issuecomment-1261467633","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/44893","id":1261467633,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM5LMHfx","user":{"login":"danielwe","id":5809017,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjU4MDkwMTc=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/5809017?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe","html_url":"https://github.com/danielwe","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2022-09-28T21:09:12Z","updated_at":"2022-09-28T21:09:12Z","author_association":"NONE","body":"Bump to maybe get this in by the 1.9 feature freeze? functions"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":8,"created_at":"2022-04-07T08:00:53Z","updated_at":"2022-09-30T18:24:23Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"draft":false,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/pulls/44893","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/44893","diff_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/44893.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/44893.patch","merged_at":null},"body":"Hi all, I was going to raise an issue but figured I might as well frame it in terms of a proposed solution.

I stumbled upon a case where I couldn't `isapprox` two GPU arrays because they hit this `zip` trying to pull elements one by one. Using `mapreduce` instead should fix the problem for these and any other array types that don't like sequential iteration.

The drawback is that this hurts performance for regular CPU arrays (#38558). The slowdown is worse in cases that admit an early short-circuit:

```julia
julia> A = randn(100000); B = copy(A); A[end] = Inf;  # No short-circuit

julia> @btime isapprox(A, B);
  172.186 μs (2 allocations: 781.30 KiB)

julia> @btime isapprox_new(A, B)
  292.771 μs (12 allocations: 879.28 KiB)

julia> A[1] = 0;  # Immediate short-circuit possible

julia> @btime isapprox(A, B);
  79.641 μs (2 allocations: 781.30 KiB)

julia> @btime isapprox_new(A, B);
  292.528 μs (12 allocations: 879.28 KiB)
```

However, note that:

* This branch is a fallback for an exceptional case
* Much worse performance can occur for unrelated reasons, for example:

```julia
julia> A[1] = Inf;  # Why does this make norm(A - B) an order of magnitude slower??

julia> @btime isapprox(A, B);
  726.410 μs (2 allocations: 781.30 KiB)

julia> @btime isapprox_new(A, B);
  966.347 μs (12 allocations: 879.28 KiB)
```

So maybe a temporary slowdown (until `mapreduce` is improved) in this exceptional branch is an acceptable price to pay for more generic code? That way GPUArrays.jl won't have to duplicate the entire method just to change one line.

~Bonus: some `mapreduce` calls in `generic_norm` are really just straightforward applications of `minimum/maximum/sum`, so I rewrote them as such for improved clarity. The behavior should be unchanged. Since this change also concerns reductions in generic linear algebra routines I thought a single PR might suffice even if this is really a separate issue.~

**About tests**

* `isapprox` isn't explicitly tested on arrays. On the other hand, it's the foundation for almost every other test, so in that sense, it's very thoroughly tested.
* ~The `generic_norm` rewrites shouldn't change behavior, so existing tests should suffice.~

EDIT: rolled back the mapreduce simplifications in `norm` since they trigger #44906 and are unrelated to the main issue here.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/44893/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/44893/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1263884763","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/44893#issuecomment-1263884763","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/44893","id":1263884763,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM5LVVnb","user":{"login":"danielwe","id":5809017,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjU4MDkwMTc=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/5809017?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe","html_url":"https://github.com/danielwe","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2022-09-30T18:24:22Z","updated_at":"2022-09-30T18:24:22Z","author_association":"NONE","body":"The buildbot log says "make: *** No rule to make target 'win-extras'. {"action":"created","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/OrdinaryDiffEq.jl/issues/1510","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/OrdinaryDiffEq.jl","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/OrdinaryDiffEq.jl/issues/1510/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/OrdinaryDiffEq.jl/issues/1510/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/OrdinaryDiffEq.jl/issues/1510/events","html_url":"https://github.com/SciML/OrdinaryDiffEq.jl/issues/1510","id":1034034209,"node_id":"I_kwDOBEVPe849ohwh","number":1510,"title":"Error when using custom Wfact or Wfact_t in SplitODEProblem","user":{"login":"dennisYatunin","id":8855258,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjg4NTUyNTg=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/8855258?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/dennisYatunin","html_url":"https://github.com/dennisYatunin","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/dennisYatunin/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/dennisYatunin/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/dennisYatunin/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/dennisYatunin/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/dennisYatunin/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/dennisYatunin/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/dennisYatunin/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/dennisYatunin/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/dennisYatunin/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":8,"created_at":"2021-10-23T01:07:10Z","updated_at":"2022-06-03T03:43:16Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"In order to use a custom `Wfact` or `Wfact_t` with a split ODE solver, one needs to specify a problem of the form `SplitODEProblem(ODEFunction(f_implicit!; Wfact_t = custom_wfact_t!), f_explicit!, u0, tspan, p)`. [This results](https://github.com/SciML/SciMLBase.jl/blob/7f81097a492ab1707209530627a494bfb10c90c5/src/problems/ode_problems.jl#L186-L228) in an `ODEProblem` that contains a `SplitFunction` `f`, where the specified custom value of `Wfact_t` (or `Wfact`) is stored in `f.f1.Wfact_t`. The corresponding field in `f`, `f.Wfact_t`, contains its [default value](https://github.com/SciML/SciMLBase.jl/blob/7f81097a492ab1707209530627a494bfb10c90c5/src/scimlfunctions.jl#L533) of `nothing`.

In order to determine whether a custom `Wfact_t` has been provided, [`has_Wfact_t(f)`](https://github.com/SciML/SciMLBase.jl/blob/7f81097a492ab1707209530627a494bfb10c90c5/src/scimlfunctions.jl#L1324) checks `f.f1.Wfact_t`, rather than `f.Wfact_t`. When computing the value of `W`, `calc_W!` [checks if `has_Wfact_t(f)` when the solver requires `Wfact_t`](https://github.com/SciML/OrdinaryDiffEq.jl/blob/3a9b6dc35e3ed19ddda4801cc5c791bf8771f956/src/derivative_utils.jl#L468) (or it [checks if  `has_Wfact(f)` when the solver requires `Wfact`](https://github.com/SciML/OrdinaryDiffEq.jl/blob/3a9b6dc35e3ed19ddda4801cc5c791bf8771f956/src/derivative_utils.jl#L473)). If the problem is specified as above, this condition is satisfied. Unfortunately, on the following line, `calc_W!` calls [`f.Wfact_t(W, u, p, dtgamma, t)`](https://github.com/SciML/OrdinaryDiffEq.jl/blob/3a9b6dc35e3ed19ddda4801cc5c791bf8771f956/src/derivative_utils.jl#L469) (or [`f.Wfact(W, u, p, dtgamma, t)`](https://github.com/SciML/OrdinaryDiffEq.jl/blob/3a9b6dc35e3ed19ddda4801cc5c791bf8771f956/src/derivative_utils.jl#L474)). Since `f.Wfact_t` is `nothing` for a `SplitFunction`, this causes an error.

There is also another issue when the problem is specified as `SplitODEProblem(ODEFunction(f_implicit!; jac_prototype = CustomWRepresentation(), Wfact_t = custom_wfact_t!), f_explicit!, u0, tspan, p)` in order to make use of a problem-specific struct for representing `W`. Just as with `Wfact` and `Wfact_t`, the custom struct is stored in `f.f1.jac_prototype`, while `f.jac_prototype` contains its [default value](https://github.com/SciML/SciMLBase.jl/blob/7f81097a492ab1707209530627a494bfb10c90c5/src/scimlfunctions.jl#L530) of `nothing`. When the algorithm cache is being built, `build_J_W` only uses a custom struct for representing `W` if [`f.jac_prototype !== nothing`](https://github.com/SciML/OrdinaryDiffEq.jl/blob/3a9b6dc35e3ed19ddda4801cc5c791bf8771f956/src/derivative_utils.jl#L606), so it ignores the custom `jac_prototype` when the problem is specified as above.

These issues can be fixed as follows:
```
import SciMLBase: SplitFunction
function SplitFunction{iip}(f1,f2; kwargs...) where iip
  f1 = ODEFunction(f1)
  f2 = ODEFunction{iip}(f2)
  SplitFunction{iip,RECOMPILE_BY_DEFAULT}(f1,f2; kwargs...,
                                          Wfact = f1.Wfact,
                                          Wfact_t = f1.Wfact_t,
                                          jac_prototype = f1.jac_prototype,
  )
end
```
However, this solution causes custom values of `Wfact`, `Wfact_t`, and `jac_prototype` to be stored in two places for split ODE problems (in both `f` and `f.f1`). An alternative approach could be to make getter functions for `Wfact`, `Wfact_t`, and `jac_prototype`, and to use those functions in `calc_W!` and `build_J_W` instead of directly accessing the fields of `f`.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/OrdinaryDiffEq.jl/issues/1510/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/OrdinaryDiffEq.jl/issues/1510/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/OrdinaryDiffEq.jl/issues/comments/1145554454","html_url":"https://github.com/SciML/OrdinaryDiffEq.jl/issues/1510#issuecomment-1145554454","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/OrdinaryDiffEq.jl/issues/1510","id":1145554454,"node_id":"IC_kwDOBEVPe85ER8YW","user":{"login":"danielwe","id":5809017,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjU4MDkwMTc=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/5809017?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe","html_url":"https://github.com/danielwe","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2022-06-03T03:43:16Z","updated_at":"2022-06-03T03:43:16Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"Just to clarify, I wasn't thinking about this feature from an efficiency perspective, as in people deliberately choosing to use SplitFunction because of an efficient way to split the Jacobian (you should just specify the jac prototype as a SciMLLinearCombination in that case, if that type survives anywhere). It's more about convenience and correctness when experimenting with different solvers, same as how a call to `f::SplitFunction` works transparently. But really, I'd applaud either choice: automatically and transparently wrapping a SciMLLinearCombination around `f1.jac` and `f2.jac`, or just not allowing `f.jac` at all. It's having an independently and manually specified `f.jac` that's dangerous.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/OrdinaryDiffEq.jl/issues/comments/1145554454/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

the mul! implementation for ScaledDiffEqOperator and AddedDiffEqOperator, and the latter no longer needs to preallocate any caches. Both of the following (and their 5-arg generalization) can be replaced by a single call to 5-arg mul! with no reference to `L.cache`:

https://github.com/SciML/SciMLOperators.jl/blob/d58961f6fed1083bf45e101703e0272a788e9582/src/operators/basic_operators.jl#L286-L287

https://github.com/SciML/SciMLOperators.jl/blob/d58961f6fed1083bf45e101703e0272a788e9582/src/operators/basic_operators.jl#L389-L390

For function-based operators, the user would ideally be able to provide a 5-arg function, and the fallback when a 3-arg function is provided could be to preallocate a cache to enable allocation-free 5-arg mul!

(This is probably all obvious. I'm just looking forward to using this package. Don't be surprised if you see some PRs down the road.)","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/SciMLOperators.jl/issues/comments/1145577429/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

so much for asking! As far as my use cases are concerned, the important points are:

* Lazy scaling, composition, and linear combination
* Straightforward updating that recurses down the operator tree
* 5-arg `mul!`
* Non-square operators (even though the full operator is usually N x N in SciML use cases, you want to be able to represent it as a low-rank decomposition (N x 2) x (2 x N))

Just skimming the code briefly but it looks like it's all there :tada:

You also have a lazy tensor product, which is awesome! I'm not using that right now, but I would have given a lot to have it a couple of years ago, and it may come back around in the future. (Tensor sums, block operators, and block diagonal operators would also be nice down the road I suppose, but they're not urgent for me at least.)

A nice-to-have would be a variant of the in-place `FunctionOperator` that can take an accumulating "5-arg" function, i.e., a function with signature `f!(y, x, α, β)` representing `y = α * L * x + β * y`. This is not something LinearMaps has, but the more I think about it the more this seems like the right way to specify an in-place matrix-free linear operator.

Huge disclaimer: I don't think I'm a very typical user, so don't count on my use cases covering the most important functionality.

---

Some final thoughts/questions, having skimmed the code:

* Curious why the algebra leans eager, such that, e.g., `*(::MatrixOperator, ::MatrixOperator)` returns a `MatrixOperator` rather than a `ComposedOperator`. For one, I actually don't think it's possible to treat `update_func` correctly this way---there's no way to fix this line, which incorrectly assumes that `f(A) * g(B) == f(AB) * g(AB)`: https://github.com/SciML/SciMLOperators.jl/blob/53ebd2cf17446ae9ff6c691fabfcac7ef8c841b1/src/sciml.jl#L83 A consistent treatment of `update_func` probably enforces laziness for every operation that returns an `AbstractSciMLOperator`. Seems like the only eager operations should be ones that return an `AbstractArray`.
* I see there's no `update_func` on `FunctionOperator`. This could be a very useful addition: you could use a callable struct to represent a non-constant matrix-free operator. (One use case that comes to mind is JVP/VJP operators, although a better solution for those is probably dedicated `{JVP,VPJ}Operator` types in a downstream library.)
* It wasn't immediately clear to me what `InvertibleOperator` should represent. Might `FactorizedOperator` be a clearer name?
* On the topic of names, a few more things, but this is getting nitpicky:
  * `AddedOperator` doesn't quite sit the way it should somehow, but I suppose the old `CombinedOperator` lacks clarity. How about `SummedOperator`? It seems quite universal to use the word "sum" about a reduction by addition.
  *  I don't think "adjoint" is a verb such that something can be "adjointed". I really appreciate the consistency in using a past participle to name every lazy wrapper, but I think "adjoint" already is the past participle of the verb "to adjoin". So to keep up the consistency I think the appropriate name is `AdjointOperator` (even though it sticks out for being the only participle that doesn't end in "ed").

That was probably more code review than you bargained for, but the main point is I'm hugely appreciative of where this is going. The timing is also impeccable---I was in the middle of making my own pirated hodgepodge of operator packages to get the functionality I want, but this library will basically solve my problem.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/SciMLOperators.jl/issues/comments/1149103442/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

This would lead to faster evaluations (`(A * B) * u` as compared to `A * (B * u)` where `A * B` has already been evaluated).

That's not generally true. Extreme example: if `A` and `B` are N x 1 and 1 x N, `A * (B * u)` is `2N` multiplications, while `(A * B) * u` is `N^2`, even after precomputing `A * B` (which is also `N^2`).

More generally I'd find it very surprising if `A * B` returns an `AbstractSciMLOperator` but doesn't respect the update functions of `A` and `B`. I think it would be a friendlier interface if one could trust that operations that map `AbstractSciMLOperator`s to `AbstractSciMLOperator`s are faithful to the interface, while eagerly computing methods return a plain array to make it clear that they represent a frozen snapshot.

That way, it's totally fine if you decide that, for example, `*` should eager and `∘` should be lazy: just return a plain array in the former case.

---

> InvertibleOperator is an operator which has the method \ or ldiv! or both

I think this statement clarifies the issue: `InvertibleOperator` sounds more like a trait than a type. Then again this is a type that just wraps anything that has the trait, so maybe that's the right name for it after all.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/SciMLOperators.jl/issues/comments/1149219274/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

For simplicity just consider polynomials `f(A)` and `g(B)`.
