,"gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe","html_url":"https://github.com/danielwe","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2022-06-08T04:27:53Z","updated_at":"2022-06-08T04:27:53Z","author_association":"NONE","body":"> for eg when one pass the operator to a krylov solver

Oh, I see where you're coming from now, you're thinking about the optimal way to "freeze" the operator at a particular timestep before passing it to an iterative solver. To me, that doesn't sound like something that's best accomplished bottom-up through the `Base.:*` methods, but rather something that calls for a top-down `freeze` function that returns a single-use fused and frozen operator and leaves its argument unchanged.

However, it would be sad if implicit ODE solvers automatically made use of an overly eager `freeze` that would fuse structures for which the disaggregated matvecmul is orders of magnitude faster, as in the example above. Though, actually, it might not be so hard to come up with good heuristics for which nodes to fuse and which to leave unchanged; the computational complexity of a matvecmul is a straightforward function of the shapes of the arrays involved. Still, for my current use cases, I'd be happier if I could simply know that the operator passed through to the iterative solver is identical to the one I instantiate. (I put a lot of thought into its structure.)

One last thought, if you decide that some binary operations should absolutely fuse, I suppose the consistent choice would be to only accept constant operators and have them throw an error if given operators with nontrivial update functions (with a helpful error message pointing to the equivalent lazy operation). Another alternative that I _don't_ think is a good idea is to let a nominally eager operation act lazily as a fallback---that would make it too hard to reason about expressions involving operators.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/SciMLOperators.jl/issues/comments/1149446517/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

order to use a custom `Wfact` or `Wfact_t` with a split ODE solver, one needs to specify a problem of the form `SplitODEProblem(ODEFunction(f_implicit!; Wfact_t = custom_wfact_t!), f_explicit!, u0, tspan, p)`. [This results](https://github.com/SciML/SciMLBase.jl/blob/7f81097a492ab1707209530627a494bfb10c90c5/src/problems/ode_problems.jl#L186-L228) in an `ODEProblem` that contains a `SplitFunction` `f`, where the specified custom value of `Wfact_t` (or `Wfact`) is stored in `f.f1.Wfact_t`. The corresponding field in `f`, `f.Wfact_t`, contains its [default value](https://github.com/SciML/SciMLBase.jl/blob/7f81097a492ab1707209530627a494bfb10c90c5/src/scimlfunctions.jl#L533) of `nothing`.

In order to determine whether a custom `Wfact_t` has been provided, [`has_Wfact_t(f)`](https://github.com/SciML/SciMLBase.jl/blob/7f81097a492ab1707209530627a494bfb10c90c5/src/scimlfunctions.jl#L1324) checks `f.f1.Wfact_t`, rather than `f.Wfact_t`. When computing the value of `W`, `calc_W!` [checks if `has_Wfact_t(f)` when the solver requires `Wfact_t`](https://github.com/SciML/OrdinaryDiffEq.jl/blob/3a9b6dc35e3ed19ddda4801cc5c791bf8771f956/src/derivative_utils.jl#L468) (or it [checks if  `has_Wfact(f)` when the solver requires `Wfact`](https://github.com/SciML/OrdinaryDiffEq.jl/blob/3a9b6dc35e3ed19ddda4801cc5c791bf8771f956/src/derivative_utils.jl#L473)). If the problem is specified as above, this condition is satisfied. Unfortunately, on the following line, `calc_W!` calls [`f.Wfact_t(W, u, p, dtgamma, t)`](https://github.com/SciML/OrdinaryDiffEq.jl/blob/3a9b6dc35e3ed19ddda4801cc5c791bf8771f956/src/derivative_utils.jl#L469) (or [`f.Wfact(W, u, p, dtgamma, t)`](https://github.com/SciML/OrdinaryDiffEq.jl/blob/3a9b6dc35e3ed19ddda4801cc5c791bf8771f956/src/derivative_utils.jl#L474)). Since `f.Wfact_t` is `nothing` for a `SplitFunction`, this causes an error.

There is also another issue when the problem is specified as `SplitODEProblem(ODEFunction(f_implicit!; jac_prototype = CustomWRepresentation(), Wfact_t = custom_wfact_t!), f_explicit!, u0, tspan, p)` in order to make use of a problem-specific struct for representing `W`. Just as with `Wfact` and `Wfact_t`, the custom struct is stored in `f.f1.jac_prototype`, while `f.jac_prototype` contains its [default value](https://github.com/SciML/SciMLBase.jl/blob/7f81097a492ab1707209530627a494bfb10c90c5/src/scimlfunctions.jl#L530) of `nothing`. When the algorithm cache is being built, `build_J_W` only uses a custom struct for representing `W` if [`f.jac_prototype !== nothing`](https://github.com/SciML/OrdinaryDiffEq.jl/blob/3a9b6dc35e3ed19ddda4801cc5c791bf8771f956/src/derivative_utils.jl#L606), so it ignores the custom `jac_prototype` when the problem is specified as above.

These issues can be fixed as follows:
```
import SciMLBase: SplitFunction
function SplitFunction{iip}(f1,f2; kwargs...) where iip
  f1 = ODEFunction(f1)
  f2 = ODEFunction{iip}(f2)
  SplitFunction{iip,RECOMPILE_BY_DEFAULT}(f1,f2; kwargs...,
                                          Wfact = f1.Wfact,
                                          Wfact_t = f1.Wfact_t,
                                          jac_prototype = f1.jac_prototype,
  )
end
```
However, this solution causes custom values of `Wfact`, `Wfact_t`, and `jac_prototype` to be stored in two places for split ODE problems (in both `f` and `f.f1`). An alternative approach could be to make getter functions for `Wfact`, `Wfact_t`, and `jac_prototype`, and to use those functions in `calc_W!` and `build_J_W` instead of directly accessing the fields of `f`.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/OrdinaryDiffEq.jl/issues/1510/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/OrdinaryDiffEq.jl/issues/1510/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/OrdinaryDiffEq.jl/issues/comments/1150471079","html_url":"https://github.com/SciML/OrdinaryDiffEq.jl/issues/1510#issuecomment-1150471079","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/OrdinaryDiffEq.jl/issues/1510","id":1150471079,"node_id":"IC_kwDOBEVPe85Eksun","user":{"login":"danielwe","id":5809017,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjU4MDkwMTc=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/5809017?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe","html_url":"https://github.com/danielwe","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2022-06-08T22:19:56Z","updated_at":"2022-06-08T22:19:56Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"> If [`f1.jac`]'s defined and someone puts this into Rodas5, what does it do?

My mental model here is that the jacobian of `F::ODEFunction` is simply called as `F.jac(u, p, t)`, which either points to a user-defined function or an autodiff-generated function. (I know that this model is not correct, it's actually `calc_J(integrator, cache)`, but bear with me.) Within this picture I see two options when someone tries to call `f.jac(u, p, t)` and `f` is a `SplitFunction`:

* Return `AddedOperator(f.f1.jac(u, p, t), f.f2.jac(u, p, t))`
* Throw an error that says that `SplitFunction` doesn't support jacobians and is thus incompatible with implicit solvers

I'd be fine with either. The latter may be the most foolproof, as the current buggy mixing of `f.jac` and `f.f1.jac` in IMEX solvers would throw an error.

In the former case, if no `f.f2.jac` was specified by the user, it falls back to autodiff the same way it would if `f.f2` alone were used in an implicit solver. How `f2` provides its jacobian is up to `f2`, it's not `SplitFunction`s business. In fact, it's the same for `f.f1`.

No cache array is needed. `AddedOperator` uses 5-arg mul! internally for operator-vector multiplication, and as far as I know, it doesn't support `ldiv!` or factorization, so it would only work with iterative linsolves.

In this mental model, the separation of concerns and the possible choices seem unambiguous. The practical implementation is hairier because all the logic about how to compute the right jacobian, depending on function type and algorithm, lives within a single `calc_J` function and subroutines like `nlsolve_f`. I'm sure this design is necessary to deal with DAEs and other things I haven't thought about, but it's harder to reason about and the bug we're discussing here is a case in point.

Whatever you decide on, just make sure `SplitFunction(f1, f2; jac=myjac)` is an error. The jacobian of `f::Splitfunction` should either be an autogenerated linear combination or an error.

---

Good point about thread safety of the fused `f` fallback. In my own code I often do `caches = ((create_cache() for _ in Threads.nthreads())...,)` and access it as `caches[Threads.threadid()]`. Could something like that be an option for `SplitFunction`?","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/OrdinaryDiffEq.jl/issues/comments/1150471079/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

You're passing it to an iterative solver. Depending on the shapes of `A` and `B`, precomputing the product `Cfused = A.A * B.A` and passing that to the solver instead could be faster in some cases. You can't define `C` this way from the outset because `A` and `B` depend on `(u, p, t)`, but you can always fuse ahead of any sequence of operations that lives on a constant `(u, p, t)`-slice.

Opt-in functionality to support this might be useful. For more complicated structures it may try to approximate the optimal solution or it may rely on a flag on each composite node to decide whether to fuse.

We touched on this in https://github.com/SciML/SciMLOperators.jl/issues/12#issuecomment-1150403367.

Low prioirity.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/SciMLOperators.jl/issues/51/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/SciMLOperators.jl/issues/51/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null}}

structs","user":{"login":"danielwe","id":5809017,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjU4MDkwMTc=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/5809017?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe","html_url":"https://github.com/danielwe","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":0,"created_at":"2022-06-16T03:41:55Z","updated_at":"2022-06-16T03:41:55Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"For leaf nodes that aren't simple array wrappers, such as `ScalarOperator` and `FunctionOperator`, DiffEqOperators.jl had mutable structs that would be changed in-place by `update_coefficients!`. In contrast, SciMLOperators.jl seems to opt for immutable structs and use Setfield.jl. Example:

https://github.com/SciML/SciMLOperators.jl/blob/7dd3a9d89273039268b650f873e6ff10ec285a27/src/sciml.jl#L372-L376

However, this is incompatible with, for example, using a SciMLOperator as the `jac_prototype` in an in-place `ODEFunction`. Lines such as the following expect the operator to be updated in-place: https://github.com/SciML/OrdinaryDiffEq.jl/blob/3076b4ef7015cc3d683a82087761d56f20ac43d9/src/derivative_utils.jl#L148

Also, if you actually want to migrate to Setfield.jl despite this issue, the approach currently looks broken for recursive updating of composite operators. The following function will throw away any out-of-place Setfield-style changes and only keep in-place changes:

However, it should be possible to apply `L(u, p, t)` to other vectors than `u`, i.e., operations `L(u, p, t) * v`. As far as I can tell, this is possible for all operator types except `FunctionOperator`. Perhaps it would make more sense for the function wrapped by `FunctionOperator` to have signature `f(v, u, p, t)` (or `f!(out, v, u, p, t)` for in-place)?\r\n\r\nOr, in combination with #48, the supported signatures would be `f(v, u, p, t, α, β)` and `f!(out, v, u, p, t, α, β)` (modulo your preferred argument order).","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/SciMLOperators.jl/issues/57/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/SciMLOperators.jl/issues/57/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null}}

```julia
using SciMLOperators, LinearAlgebra
using BenchmarkTools

A = TensorProductOperator(rand(12,12), rand(12,12), rand(12,12))

u = rand(12^3, 100)
v = rand(12^3, 100)

A = cache_operator(A, u)

mul!(v, A, u) # dunny
@btime mul!($v, $A, $u); # 16.901 ms (348801 allocations: 45.51 MiB)

B = convert(AbstractMatrix, A)
mul!(v, B, u) # dummy
@btime mul!($v, $B, $u); # 10.337 ms (0 allocations: 0 bytes)
```

{"action":"created","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/SciMLOperators.jl/issues/56","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/SciMLOperators.jl","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/SciMLOperators.jl/issues/56/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/SciMLOperators.jl/issues/56/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/SciMLOperators.jl/issues/56/events","html_url":"https://github.com/SciML/SciMLOperators.jl/issues/56","id":1273008653,"node_id":"I_kwDOHZ-dpc5L4JIN","number":56,"title":"`update_coefficients!` currently broken, probably needs mutable structs","user":{"login":"danielwe","id":5809017,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjU4MDkwMTc=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/5809017?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe","html_url":"https://github.com/danielwe","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":1,"created_at":"2022-06-16T03:41:55Z","updated_at":"2022-06-18T20:01:21Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"For leaf nodes that aren't simple array wrappers, such as `ScalarOperator` and `FunctionOperator`, DiffEqOperators.jl had mutable structs that would be changed in-place by `update_coefficients!`. In contrast, SciMLOperators.jl seems to opt for immutable structs and use Setfield.jl. Example:

https://github.com/SciML/SciMLOperators.jl/blob/7dd3a9d89273039268b650f873e6ff10ec285a27/src/sciml.jl#L372-L376

However, this is incompatible with, for example, using a SciMLOperator as the `jac_prototype` in an in-place `ODEFunction`. Lines such as the following expect the operator to be updated in-place: https://github.com/SciML/OrdinaryDiffEq.jl/blob/3076b4ef7015cc3d683a82087761d56f20ac43d9/src/derivative_utils.jl#L148

Also, if you actually want to migrate to Setfield.jl despite this issue, the approach currently looks broken for recursive updating of composite operators. The following function will throw away any out-of-place Setfield-style changes and only keep in-place changes:

https://github.com/SciML/SciMLOperators.jl/blob/7dd3a9d89273039268b650f873e6ff10ec285a27/src/interface.jl#L22-L27","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/SciMLOperators.jl/issues/56/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/SciMLOperators.jl/issues/56/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/SciMLOperators.jl/issues/comments/1159551240","html_url":"https://github.com/SciML/SciMLOperators.jl/issues/56#issuecomment-1159551240","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/SciMLOperators.jl/issues/56","id":1159551240,"node_id":"IC_kwDOHZ-dpc5FHVkI","user":{"login":"danielwe","id":5809017,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjU4MDkwMTc=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/5809017?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe","html_url":"https://github.com/danielwe","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2022-06-18T20:01:21Z","updated_at":"2022-06-18T20:01:21Z","author_association":"NONE","body":"I had a brief slack discussion with @ChrisRackauckas back when I was trying to make LinearMaps work for my Jacobian. I had written functions to update the wrapped arrays, but couldn't update the scalar in a ScaledMap because ScaledMap is an immutable struct. Chris confirmed that if `f` is an in-place ODEFunction, the DifferentialEquations API requires actually mutating `J` inside `f.jac(J, u, p, t)`, and there's no plan or desire to change this to use the return value instead, or otherwise support hybrid ODEFunctions with in-place `f` and out-of-place `jac`. The solution I was presented with was to migrate from LinearMaps to DiffEqOperators precisely because they are mutable across the board. So unless that sentiment has changed and there's a plan to change the DifferentialEquations API, I really think SciMLOperators need to be mutable.

Though I'm curious which of the types actually need to be mutable structs to accomplish this. `ScalarOperator` and `FunctionOperator` are obvious because you need in-place updating of the scalar and `(u, p, t)`. `MatrixOperator` is less clear; it usually wraps a mutable array which is what you actually want to update, but I suppose it could also wrap a `StaticArray`, so in principle, it would have to be a mutable struct to make sure it always supports in-place updating, though that seems like a strange way to use it. I guess all the composition operators can remain immutable as long as updating only means updating data, not structure.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/SciMLOperators.jl/issues/comments/1159551240/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

Actually redefining `update_coefficients!` is probably not meaningful, but replacing `p` with `(u, p)` and updating your function to work with that would do the job.

However, it's a bit of a jarring break with the very consistent `(u, p, t)` interface across the rest of the ecosystem, where `u` and `p` always refer to the same thing. It feels like if the signature for an in-place jacobian were `jac!(J, p, t)` and you had to use `p` as a vehicle if you wanted `J` to depend on `u`.

If this is going to be the sanctioned way to separate the state that your operator depends on from the vector it acts on, there should probably be an example in the docs to illustrate how to do it.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/SciMLOperators.jl/issues/comments/1159553759/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

actually, it doesn't work, because `update_coefficients!` is called recursively on the nodes in the operator tree, so the arguments have to have the same meaning everywhere. The only way for the user to customize how `FunctionOperator` is updated would be to redefine its `update_coefficients!` method, which would be type piracy and wouldn't be able to differentiate between different instances (the alternative would be for `FunctionOperator` to accept a `update_func` argument, but that's messy because it would require the user to deal with the internals of the struct).

Hence I maintain that the appropriate signatures for the function wrapped by `FunctionOperator` are `f(v, u, p, t)` and `f!(w, v, u, p, t)`. There needs to be a distinction between the argument the function acts on as an operator and the ones it depends on as parameters, and `u` is always one of the latter (even if it _can_ also be the former). You should be able to do the following for `FunctionOperator` and get the result you expect, just like you can for any other operator:

```
update_coefficients!(L, u, p, t)
w = L * v
```

(Of course, calling the operator as a function should dispatch to a call where `u` plays both roles: `L(u, p, t) = f(u, u, p, t)`, equivalent to how it works for every other operator).","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/SciMLOperators.jl/issues/comments/1159557816/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

structs","user":{"login":"danielwe","id":5809017,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjU4MDkwMTc=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/5809017?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe","html_url":"https://github.com/danielwe","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":4,"created_at":"2022-06-16T03:41:55Z","updated_at":"2022-06-20T19:57:11Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"For leaf nodes that aren't simple array wrappers, such as `ScalarOperator` and `FunctionOperator`, DiffEqOperators.jl had mutable structs that would be changed in-place by `update_coefficients!`. In contrast, SciMLOperators.jl seems to opt for immutable structs and use Setfield.jl. Example:

https://github.com/SciML/SciMLOperators.jl/blob/7dd3a9d89273039268b650f873e6ff10ec285a27/src/sciml.jl#L372-L376

However, this is incompatible with, for example, using a SciMLOperator as the `jac_prototype` in an in-place `ODEFunction`. Lines such as the following expect the operator to be updated in-place: https://github.com/SciML/OrdinaryDiffEq.jl/blob/3076b4ef7015cc3d683a82087761d56f20ac43d9/src/derivative_utils.jl#L148

Also, if you actually want to migrate to Setfield.jl despite this issue, the approach currently looks broken for recursive updating of composite operators. The following function will throw away any out-of-place Setfield-style changes and only keep in-place changes:

https://github.com/SciML/SciMLOperators.jl/blob/7dd3a9d89273039268b650f873e6ff10ec285a27/src/interface.jl#L22-L27","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/SciMLOperators.jl/issues/56/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/SciMLOperators.jl/issues/56/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/SciMLOperators.jl/issues/comments/1160799804","html_url":"https://github.com/SciML/SciMLOperators.jl/issues/56#issuecomment-1160799804","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/SciMLOperators.jl/issues/56","id":1160799804,"node_id":"IC_kwDOHZ-dpc5FMGY8","user":{"login":"danielwe","id":5809017,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjU4MDkwMTc=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/5809017?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe","html_url":"https://github.com/danielwe","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2022-06-20T19:57:11Z","updated_at":"2022-06-20T19:57:11Z","author_association":"NONE","body":"> immutable structs aren't completely incompatible with in-place updates

Absolutely, if the struct wraps something mutable you can just mutate that. The issue is with structs that wrap immutable stuff, such as `ScalarOperator` and `FunctionOperator`. (There's also the question of what to do when a `MatrixOperator` wraps a `StaticArray`, but that seems much less important in practice.)

> For `ScalarOperator`, we can always just dump the scalar in an array, and access it via `Base.first`.

> have an inner constructor where if parameters and time are given they are `Ref(p)`'d

When I've profiled this, a mutable struct always performs better than an immutable struct wrapping a `RefValue` or size-1 array (granted, with `RefValue` the difference is small, but it's reproducible).

> maybe we can wrap the parameters in `ComponentArrays` or some other structure that can be updated in place.

That's a huge constraint on what I can put in `p` that would only kick in for workflows that use this package. And again, a mutable struct performs better.

In general, I think it's important to keep in mind the context here. Unless I've misunderstood, this package is supposed to integrate fully with SciML workflows. In that case, mutability is simply required for compatibility with how the rest of the ecosystem is designed. (Of course, you're the ones designing and developing the package, I'm just a pestering user with high hopes for what I'll be able to do with it. Hope to find time to contribute PRs soon.)","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/SciMLOperators.jl/issues/comments/1160799804/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

{"action":"opened","number":21,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/PreallocationTools.jl/pulls/21","id":929434601,"node_id":"PR_kwDOF07Nxs43Zgvp","html_url":"https://github.com/SciML/PreallocationTools.jl/pull/21","diff_url":"https://github.com/SciML/PreallocationTools.jl/pull/21.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/SciML/PreallocationTools.jl/pull/21.patch","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/PreallocationTools.jl/issues/21","number":21,"state":"open","locked":false,"title":"Fix issues with LazyBufferCache","user":{"login":"danielwe","id":5809017,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjU4MDkwMTc=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/5809017?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe","html_url":"https://github.com/danielwe","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"This PR fixes some bugs and aligns code and documentation for LazyBufferCache. In order of commits:

* `b = LazyBufferCache(f)` produced caches of arbitrary lengths when `f` was a closure.
  * MWE:
    ```julia
    using PreallocationTools

    LBC(n) = LazyBufferCache(_ -> n)

    b = LBC(5)
    b[ones(1)]  # Arbitrary length or OOM, should be length 5
    ```
  * Cause: `f` was not being passed through to `new` in the constructor, making `b.lengthmap` an uninitialized instance of `typeof(f)`. (I'm surprised this is even allowed.)
  * Solution: Pass `f` through to `new`
* `b[x]` and `b[y]` would return the same buffer as long as they have the same eltype, regardless of sizes and array types/storage
  * MWE:
    ```julia
    using CUDA
    using PreallocationTools

    b = LazyBufferCache()
    x = ones(4)
    b[x]  # Length 4 as expected
    b[x[1:3]]  # Still length 4, should be length 3
    b[CuArray(x[1:3])]  # Still trying to return a CPU array of length 4 => typeassert error in getindex; should be CuArray of length 3
    ```
  * Cause: only `eltype(x)` used as key to `b.bufs`, not size and array type
  * Solution: use `(typeof(x), f(size(x)))` as key
  * Outstanding issue: the typeassert that ensures output type inferability relies on `typeof(similar(u, size)) === typeof(u)`. This does not hold for all `AbstractArray`s; ranges like `1:5` are the canonical counterexamples. This hardly seems relevant for LazyBufferCache use cases, so I've left it as is.
* Docs and behavior inconsistent: `f` is actually used as size map, not length map, so the LBC can both accept and return arrays of arbitrary dimension, not just vectors. In particular, matrices are already supported, contrary to what a comment in the code claimed.
  * Solution: Update docs and variable names, e.g., from `b.lengthmap` to `b.sizemap`.

Some tests are probably in order, I'll get to that {"action":"created","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/24943","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/24943/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/24943/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/24943/events","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/24943","id":279726697,"node_id":"MDU6SXNzdWUyNzk3MjY2OTc=","number":24943,"title":"Warn on uninitialized isbits-fields in structs?","user":{"login":"KristofferC","id":1282691,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjEyODI2OTE=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/1282691?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/KristofferC","html_url":"https://github.com/KristofferC","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KristofferC/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KristofferC/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KristofferC/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KristofferC/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KristofferC/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KristofferC/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KristofferC/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KristofferC/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KristofferC/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":4,"created_at":"2017-12-06T11:57:13Z","updated_at":"2022-05-17T00:03:06Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"MEMBER","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"For example:

```
julia> struct S
           a::Int
           b::Bool
           S(a::Int, b::Bool) = new(a)
       end

julia> S(1, false)
S(1, true)
```

Would it make any sense to warn that the struct `S` is being created with an uninitialized isbits-field?
The problem is that this will act like a well formed type but there is garbage memory in `S.b`. It seems like an easy mistake: add a field to the struct, forget to update the `new` call, use garbage memory that might work in 99% of the cases. Perhaps there could be a way to enforce being explicit about using uninitialized isbits field, like `new(a, uninitialized)` or something...
","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/24943/reactions","total_count":3,"+1":3,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/24943/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1128252767","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/24943#issuecomment-1128252767","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/24943","id":1128252767,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM5DP8Vf","user":{"login":"danielwe","id":5809017,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjU4MDkwMTc=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/5809017?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe","html_url":"https://github.com/danielwe","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2022-05-17T00:03:06Z","updated_at":"2022-05-17T00:03:06Z","author_association":"NONE","body":"I've stumbled upon two cases in the last 10 days where a struct field was unintentionally left uninitialized in packages within a mainstream ecosystem: https://github.com/SciML/PreallocationTools.jl/pull/21 and https://github.com/SciML/DiffEqOperators.jl/issues/519.

Deprecating the current syntax for uninitialized fields in favor of `new(..., undef, ...)` would be a great improvement. Seems to me that uninitialized data is something that should only be provided when explicitly requested (like for arrays), not something you can get without warning due to a typo while rapidly iterating on a struct layout.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1128252767/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

{"action":"created","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/24943","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/24943/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/24943/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/24943/events","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/24943","id":279726697,"node_id":"MDU6SXNzdWUyNzk3MjY2OTc=","number":24943,"title":"Warn on uninitialized isbits-fields in structs?","user":{"login":"KristofferC","id":1282691,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjEyODI2OTE=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/1282691?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/KristofferC","html_url":"https://github.com/KristofferC","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KristofferC/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KristofferC/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KristofferC/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KristofferC/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KristofferC/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KristofferC/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KristofferC/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KristofferC/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KristofferC/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":6,"created_at":"2017-12-06T11:57:13Z","updated_at":"2022-05-17T15:23:17Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"MEMBER","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"For example:

```
julia> struct S
           a::Int
           b::Bool
           S(a::Int, b::Bool) = new(a)
       end

julia> S(1, false)
S(1, true)
```

Would it make any sense to warn that the struct `S` is being created with an uninitialized isbits-field?
The problem is that this will act like a well formed type but there is garbage memory in `S.b`. It seems like an easy mistake: add a field to the struct, forget to update the `new` call, use garbage memory that might work in 99% of the cases. Perhaps there could be a way to enforce being explicit about using uninitialized isbits field, like `new(a, uninitialized)` or something...
","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/24943/reactions","total_count":3,"+1":3,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/24943/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1129007018","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/24943#issuecomment-1129007018","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/24943","id":1129007018,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM5DS0eq","user":{"login":"danielwe","id":5809017,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjU4MDkwMTc=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/5809017?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe","html_url":"https://github.com/danielwe","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2022-05-17T15:23:17Z","updated_at":"2022-05-17T15:23:17Z","author_association":"NONE","body":"I didn't mean to fire any shots, @ChrisRackauckas. MWE:

```julia
julia> using DiffEqNoiseProcess

julia> using DiffEqNoiseProcess: NoiseProblem, solve

julia> prob = NoiseProblem(WienerProcess(0.0, [0.0im]), (0.0, 1.0));

julia> sol = solve(prob; dt=0.1);

julia> sol.W[end]
1-element Vector{ComplexF64}:
 2.3820176552198165 + 0.0im
```

The scalar-valued equivalent produces complex noise as expected:

```julia
julia> prob = NoiseProblem(WienerProcess(0.0, 0.0im), (0.0, 1.0));

julia> sol = solve(prob; dt=0.1);

julia> sol.W[end]
-1.1828100925799103 + 1.4213457901396127im
```

---

Note that this doesn't seem to affect the default noise process for complex-valued SDEs in StochasticDiffEq. I'm just not able to manually instantiate a noise process that reproduces the default behavior:

```julia
julia> using StochasticDiffEq

julia> f!(du, u, p, t) = (du .= 0)
f! (generic function with 1 method)

julia> g!(du, u, p, t) = (du .= 1)
g! (generic function with 1 method)

julia> prob = SDEProblem(f!, g!, [0.0im], (0.0, 1.0));

julia> sol = solve(prob, EM(); dt=0.1);

julia> sol.u[end]
1-element Vector{ComplexF64}:
 -0.9054409033854848 - 0.4148766416920265im
```","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/DiffEqNoiseProcess.jl/issues/101/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/DiffEqNoiseProcess.jl/issues/101/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/DiffEqNoiseProcess.jl/issues/comments/1129089936","html_url":"https://github.com/SciML/DiffEqNoiseProcess.jl/issues/101#issuecomment-1129089936","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/DiffEqNoiseProcess.jl/issues/101","id":1129089936,"node_id":"IC_kwDOBUr3485DTIuQ","user":{"login":"danielwe","id":5809017,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjU4MDkwMTc=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/5809017?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe","html_url":"https://github.com/danielwe","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2022-05-17T16:42:20Z","updated_at":"2022-05-17T16:42:20Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"Oh, I see, the in-place `WienerProcess!` works. {"action":"opened","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/DiffEqNoiseProcess.jl/issues/102","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/DiffEqNoiseProcess.jl","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/DiffEqNoiseProcess.jl/issues/102/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/DiffEqNoiseProcess.jl/issues/102/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/DiffEqNoiseProcess.jl/issues/102/events","html_url":"https://github.com/SciML/DiffEqNoiseProcess.jl/issues/102","id":1238921425,"node_id":"I_kwDOBUr3485J2HDR","number":102,"title":"`wiener_randn!` needs GPU-friendly fallback for complex AbstractArrays","user":{"login":"danielwe","id":5809017,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjU4MDkwMTc=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/5809017?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe","html_url":"https://github.com/danielwe","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":0,"created_at":"2022-05-17T16:57:40Z","updated_at":"2022-05-17T16:57:40Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"This method will process GPUArrays sequentially:

https://github.com/SciML/DiffEqNoiseProcess.jl/blob/c3f3bb9bbb9b7cce41804f6847d0011f252daf2d/src/wiener.jl#L18-L23

Should be tightened to `Array` and supplemented with a fallback, like in the real case.

However, looking at how the fallback for the real case works,

https://github.com/SciML/DiffEqNoiseProcess.jl/blob/c3f3bb9bbb9b7cce41804f6847d0011f252daf2d/src/wiener.jl#L15-L16

* Push to have the community (and not just SciML) consolidate around one canonical package for lazy linear map composition. To me, LinearMaps looks like the best candidate, but it needs to support the update mechanism that DiffEqOperators offers, i.e., registering update functions on the leaf operators and calling them recursively from the root. It would also be useful if function maps allowed extra arguments stored on the object (closures break updatability; requiring that the user define a callable type is fine but less friendly).
* Upstream jacvec and hesvec functions to ForwardDiff, based on the ones in SparseDiffTools.
* DiffEqBase could then define a JacVec operator that's just a LinearMaps.FunctionMap wrapping the jacvec function from ForwardDiff (and a VecJac that wraps a Zygote pullback).
* I don't know anything about the PDE discretization stuff but sounds like MethodOfLines is the final destination for that.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/DiffEqOperators.jl/issues/comments/1129320943/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

Gives me a better idea of how to get my stuff up and running in the present state. Hope to come back and help out with the transition once I've got things working.

I understand that you want most users to not have to interact directly with the operator facilities, but they are a fantastic tool to have available sometimes, so it would be great to have them exposed and explained in user-facing documentation eventually.

> there aren't many use cases where a user should need to directly use it themselves

My example is a term like `B * h(u, p, t)` where `B` is a state-independent matrix (typically tall and narrow; the low rank is why this factorization of the term makes sense; think of it as a feedback control term for a high-dim system with only a few control channels). Using ForwardDiff to turn the entire term into a JVP-operator means propagating dual numbers through the matmul, which means using a generic implementation and doing redundant flops. Specifying the jacobian prototype by hand means you can use a non-square JVP operator for the `h(u, p, t)`-factor only, and compose it with `B`. Now you have a float matmul that hits BLAS. Of course, this is not strictly needed, it's just an optimization, but a pretty quick and straightforward one if a JVP operator and operator composition are easily accessible (and it's the reason I've been talking about the need for non-square JVP functions and operators).

(Not sure there's any need a for separate JVP operator type though? If a JVP function and a matrix free operator type both exist, seems like all you'd need is a helper function to wrap the latter around the former.)","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/DiffEqOperators.jl/issues/comments/1132500303/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

{"action":"created","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/OrdinaryDiffEq.jl/issues/1510","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/OrdinaryDiffEq.jl","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/OrdinaryDiffEq.jl/issues/1510/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/OrdinaryDiffEq.jl/issues/1510/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/OrdinaryDiffEq.jl/issues/1510/events","html_url":"https://github.com/SciML/OrdinaryDiffEq.jl/issues/1510","id":1034034209,"node_id":"I_kwDOBEVPe849ohwh","number":1510,"title":"Error when using custom Wfact or Wfact_t in SplitODEProblem","user":{"login":"dennisYatunin","id":8855258,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjg4NTUyNTg=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/8855258?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/dennisYatunin","html_url":"https://github.com/dennisYatunin","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/dennisYatunin/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/dennisYatunin/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/dennisYatunin/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/dennisYatunin/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/dennisYatunin/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/dennisYatunin/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/dennisYatunin/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/dennisYatunin/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/dennisYatunin/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":6,"created_at":"2021-10-23T01:07:10Z","updated_at":"2022-05-27T18:39:29Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"In order to use a custom `Wfact` or `Wfact_t` with a split ODE solver, one needs to specify a problem of the form `SplitODEProblem(ODEFunction(f_implicit!; Wfact_t = custom_wfact_t!), f_explicit!, u0, tspan, p)`. [This results](https://github.com/SciML/SciMLBase.jl/blob/7f81097a492ab1707209530627a494bfb10c90c5/src/problems/ode_problems.jl#L186-L228) in an `ODEProblem` that contains a `SplitFunction` `f`, where the specified custom value of `Wfact_t` (or `Wfact`) is stored in `f.f1.Wfact_t`. The corresponding field in `f`, `f.Wfact_t`, contains its [default value](https://github.com/SciML/SciMLBase.jl/blob/7f81097a492ab1707209530627a494bfb10c90c5/src/scimlfunctions.jl#L533) of `nothing`.

In order to determine whether a custom `Wfact_t` has been provided, [`has_Wfact_t(f)`](https://github.com/SciML/SciMLBase.jl/blob/7f81097a492ab1707209530627a494bfb10c90c5/src/scimlfunctions.jl#L1324) checks `f.f1.Wfact_t`, rather than `f.Wfact_t`. When computing the value of `W`, `calc_W!` [checks if `has_Wfact_t(f)` when the solver requires `Wfact_t`](https://github.com/SciML/OrdinaryDiffEq.jl/blob/3a9b6dc35e3ed19ddda4801cc5c791bf8771f956/src/derivative_utils.jl#L468) (or it [checks if  `has_Wfact(f)` when the solver requires `Wfact`](https://github.com/SciML/OrdinaryDiffEq.jl/blob/3a9b6dc35e3ed19ddda4801cc5c791bf8771f956/src/derivative_utils.jl#L473)). If the problem is specified as above, this condition is satisfied. Unfortunately, on the following line, `calc_W!` calls [`f.Wfact_t(W, u, p, dtgamma, t)`](https://github.com/SciML/OrdinaryDiffEq.jl/blob/3a9b6dc35e3ed19ddda4801cc5c791bf8771f956/src/derivative_utils.jl#L469) (or [`f.Wfact(W, u, p, dtgamma, t)`](https://github.com/SciML/OrdinaryDiffEq.jl/blob/3a9b6dc35e3ed19ddda4801cc5c791bf8771f956/src/derivative_utils.jl#L474)). Since `f.Wfact_t` is `nothing` for a `SplitFunction`, this causes an error.

There is also another issue when the problem is specified as `SplitODEProblem(ODEFunction(f_implicit!; jac_prototype = CustomWRepresentation(), Wfact_t = custom_wfact_t!), f_explicit!, u0, tspan, p)` in order to make use of a problem-specific struct for representing `W`. Just as with `Wfact` and `Wfact_t`, the custom struct is stored in `f.f1.jac_prototype`, while `f.jac_prototype` contains its [default value](https://github.com/SciML/SciMLBase.jl/blob/7f81097a492ab1707209530627a494bfb10c90c5/src/scimlfunctions.jl#L530) of `nothing`. When the algorithm cache is being built, `build_J_W` only uses a custom struct for representing `W` if [`f.jac_prototype !== nothing`](https://github.com/SciML/OrdinaryDiffEq.jl/blob/3a9b6dc35e3ed19ddda4801cc5c791bf8771f956/src/derivative_utils.jl#L606), so it ignores the custom `jac_prototype` when the problem is specified as above.

These issues can be fixed as follows:
```
import SciMLBase: SplitFunction
function SplitFunction{iip}(f1,f2; kwargs...) where iip
  f1 = ODEFunction(f1)
  f2 = ODEFunction{iip}(f2)
  SplitFunction{iip,RECOMPILE_BY_DEFAULT}(f1,f2; kwargs...,
                                          Wfact = f1.Wfact,
                                          Wfact_t = f1.Wfact_t,
                                          jac_prototype = f1.jac_prototype,
  )
end
```
However, this solution causes custom values of `Wfact`, `Wfact_t`, and `jac_prototype` to be stored in two places for split ODE problems (in both `f` and `f.f1`). An alternative approach could be to make getter functions for `Wfact`, `Wfact_t`, and `jac_prototype`, and to use those functions in `calc_W!` and `build_J_W` instead of directly accessing the fields of For everything to work seamlessly with both IMEX and non-IMEX solvers and both manual and autodiff-generated jacobians, maybe the cleanest solution would be to disallow a custom `f.jac` and rather automatically wrap `f.f1.jac` and `f.f2.jac` and basically make `f.jac = f.f1.jac + f.f2.jac` (conceptually, not literal code). Basically the same way a call to `f::SplitFunction` turns into a call to `f.f1` and `f.f2` and adds them up. That way, `f.f1` and `f.f2` can each contain manual or autodiff-generated jacobians, and the combined function can just work in all types of solvers (conditional on the nlsolve code being fixed of course, i.e., the `integrator.f` -> `nlsolve_f(integrator)` substitutions).","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/OrdinaryDiffEq.jl/issues/comments/1139931809/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

stdlib"}],"state":"closed","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":56,"created_at":"2019-09-11T10:13:40Z","updated_at":"2022-04-07T01:11:34Z","closed_at":"2021-06-30T02:28:03Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"The [current implementation](https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/blob/4c8f8faa2b109c754e18a2c285d6e7aade886fdb/stdlib/Random/src/generation.jl#L29) of samplers for eg `Float23` and `Float64` effectively generates numbers as `n*eps(T)`, where `n` is an integer between `0` and `1/eps(T)-1` (inclusive). Eg for `Float64` this range is `0:(2^52-1)`.

This makes the random draws (slightly) *biased*, by `-eps(T)/2`.

The proposal is to add this term back to the result in the implementation.

Cf [this discussion](https://discourse.julialang.org/t/is-it-really-possible-for-rand-to-throw-a-zero/28505/), especially the [proposal of](https://discourse.julialang.org/t/is-it-really-possible-for-rand-to-throw-a-zero/28505/44) for adding to this old, long, and closed thread, but I encountered an example that I think is relevant context for the discussion: cuRAND, the rng library bundled with CUDA, samples uniform floats with the following properties:

* The interval is (0, 1], i.e., zero is excluded and 1 is included. See https://docs.nvidia.com/cuda/curand/host-api-overview.html#generation-functions
* The transformation from bits to float uses all 32/64 bits of the underlying UInt32/64, such that the smallest possible value is `2f0^-33`/`2.0^-65`. Hard to know exactly how carefully they are about rounding as it's not open source. Hopefully they're doing it right.

More elaboration here: https://github.com/JuliaGPU/CUDA.jl/issues/1464#issuecomment-1090893798 (note that I hadn't read this thread yet so I was a bit ignorant about the subtleties of rounding).","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1090976878/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

{"action":"opened","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/44887","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/44887/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/44887/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/44887/events","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/44887","id":1195370968,"node_id":"I_kwDOABkWpM5HP-nY","number":44887,"title":"Fallback `rand(Float32/64)` discards more entropy than necessary","user":{"login":"danielwe","id":5809017,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjU4MDkwMTc=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/5809017?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe","html_url":"https://github.com/danielwe","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":0,"created_at":"2022-04-07T01:36:18Z","updated_at":"2022-04-07T01:36:18Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"I just noticed that the current default Xoshiro RNG generates uniform floats with more entropy than other RNGs. The reason appears to be that it has it's own 24/53-bit implementation of the conversion from random bits to uniform floats at https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/blob/701468127a335d130e968a8a180021e4287abf3f/stdlib/Random/src/Xoshiro.jl#L207-L211, while the generic conversion uses a bit twiddling 23/52-bit implementation at https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/blob/701468127a335d130e968a8a180021e4287abf3f/stdlib/Random/src/generation.jl#L29-L35

The explanation for this is here:

> We still use the 52-bit version for our older RNGs so that they give the same values as before, but for the new xoshiro generator we're using shift-by-11-and-multiply currently.
> 
> _Originally posted by @JeffBezanson in https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/33222#issuecomment-859892165_

However, one consequence is that the 23/52-bit conversions are also used by packages that implement their own RNG and provide only the minimal bit sampling interface, relying on generic fallbacks to produce floats. This includes, e.g., the native RNGs in CUDA.jl and StableRNGs.jl, but also any RNG library developed in the future.

Not sure if this is intended or unintended? I suppose it's desirable in the case of StableRNGs, but not so much in the case of future implementations. Wouldn't it be better to have the 24/53-bit conversion be the generic fallback, and rather specialize to the 23/52-bit conversion for legacy RNGs? StableRNGs could implement its own conversion if it wants to promise cross-version stability of the float sequences.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/44887/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/44887/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null}}

{"action":"opened","number":44893,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/pulls/44893","id":902413511,"node_id":"PR_kwDOABkWpM41ybzH","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/44893","diff_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/44893.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/44893.patch","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/44893","number":44893,"state":"open","locked":false,"title":"Make array `isapprox` more generic by avoiding zip/explicit iteration","user":{"login":"danielwe","id":5809017,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjU4MDkwMTc=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/5809017?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe","html_url":"https://github.com/danielwe","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"Hi all, I was going to raise an issue but figured I might as well frame it in terms of a proposed solution.

I stumbled upon a case where I couldn't `isapprox` two GPU arrays because they hit this `zip` trying to pull elements one by one. Using `mapreduce` instead should fix the problem for these and any other array types that don't like sequential iteration.

The drawback is that this is a backward step in terms of performance for regular CPU arrays (#38558). The slowdown is worse in cases that admit an early short-circuit:

```julia
julia> A = randn(100000); B = copy(A); A[end] = Inf;  # No short-circuit

julia> @btime isapprox(A, B);
  172.186 μs (2 allocations: 781.30 KiB)

julia> @btime isapprox_new(A, B)
  292.771 μs (12 allocations: 879.28 KiB)

julia> A[1] = 0;  # Immediate short-circuit possible

julia> @btime isapprox(A, B);
  79.641 μs (2 allocations: 781.30 KiB)

julia> @btime isapprox_new(A, B);
  292.528 μs (12 allocations: 879.28 KiB)
```

However, note that:

* This branch is a fallback for an exceptional case
* Much worse performance can occur for unrelated reasons, for example:

```julia
julia> A[1] = Inf;  # Why does this make norm(A - B) an order of magnitude slower??

julia> @btime isapprox(A, B);
  726.410 μs (2 allocations: 781.30 KiB)

julia> @btime isapprox_new(A, B);
  966.347 μs (12 allocations: 879.28 KiB)
```

So maybe a temporary slowdown (until `mapreduce` is improved) in this exceptional branch is an acceptable price to pay for more generic code? That way GPUArrays.jl won't have to duplicate the entire method just to change one line.

Bonus: some `mapreduce` calls in `generic_norm` are really just straightforward applications of `minimum/maximum/sum`, so I rewrote them as such for improved clarity. The behavior should be unchanged. Since this change also concerns reductions in generic linear algebra routines I thought a single PR might suffice even if this is really a separate issue.

**About tests**

* `isapprox` isn't explicitly tested on arrays. On the other hand, it's the foundation for almost every other test, so in that sense, it's very thoroughly tested.

     blk

+"""
+    @invoke f(arg::T, ...; kwargs...)
+
+Provides a convenient way to call [`invoke`](@ref);
+`@invoke f(arg1::T1, arg2::T2; kwargs...)` will be expanded into `invoke(f, Tuple{T1,T2}, arg1, arg2; kwargs...)`.
+When an argument's type annotation is omitted, it's specified as `Any` argument, e.g.","path":"base/util.jl","position":9,"original_position":9,"commit_id":"ed3df19af96180296cd798f6ce803f0a126ec735","original_commit_id":"ed3df19af96180296cd798f6ce803f0a126ec735","user":{"login":"danielwe","id":5809017,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjU4MDkwMTc=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/5809017?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe","html_url":"https://github.com/danielwe","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"I realize this is merged and out in the wild, but I'd like to express support for applying ordinary dispatch rules to arguments without specified types within `@invoke`. Here's an example of why, distilled from a real-world use case:

```julia
abstract type Foo end
struct Bar <: Foo end

f(x::Foo, y) = 1
f(x::Foo, ::Nothing) = 2
f(x::Bar, y) = 3
f(x::Bar, ::Nothing) = 4

x = Bar()
Base.@invoke f(x::Foo, nothing)  # should return 2, returns 1
```

I think consistent dispatch is more important than avoiding dynamic dispatch here. Unless there's some subtlety I don't understand, the dispatch of `@invoke f(x::Foo, y)` would only have to be dynamic when the type of `y` can't be inferred, in which case `f(x, y)` and any other dispatch on the type of `y` would also be dynamic, so there's not much lost. (However, I know nothing about the gotchas with `Core.Typeof`. Suppose the type of `x` is not inferred: is `invoke(f, Tuple{Core.Typeof(x)}, x)` much slower than `f(x)`?)","created_at":"2022-04-07T08:46:11Z","updated_at":"2022-04-07T08:46:11Z","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/38438#discussion_r844879122","pull_request_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/pulls/38438","author_association":"NONE","_links":{"self":{"href":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/pulls/comments/844879122"},"html":{"href":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/38438#discussion_r844879122"},"pull_request":{"href":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/pulls/38438"}},"reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/pulls/comments/844879122/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"start_line":null,"original_start_line":null,"start_side":null,"line":523,"original_line":523,"side":"RIGHT","in_reply_to_id":549028800},"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/pulls/38438","id":520978914,"node_id":"MDExOlB1bGxSZXF1ZXN0NTIwOTc4OTE0","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/38438","diff_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/38438.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/38438.patch","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/38438","number":38438,"state":"closed","locked":false,"title":"define {"action":"opened","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/44894","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/44894/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/44894/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/44894/events","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/44894","id":1195757206,"node_id":"I_kwDOABkWpM5HRc6W","number":44894,"title":"`map!` fails to vectorize with one source array but succeeds with two","user":{"login":"danielwe","id":5809017,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjU4MDkwMTc=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/5809017?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe","html_url":"https://github.com/danielwe","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":0,"created_at":"2022-04-07T09:17:25Z","updated_at":"2022-04-07T09:17:25Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"The following two uses of `map!` are doing exactly same work, but the one that's given an extra, unused array is 4 times as fast.
 
```julia
julia> f1(xi) = xi^2;

julia> f2(xi, yi) = xi^2;

julia> N = 4096; x = randn(N); y = randn(N); z = similar(x);

julia> @btime map!(f1, $z, $x);
  2.442 μs (0 allocations: 0 bytes)

julia> @btime map!(f2, $z, $x, $y);
  625.954 ns (0 allocations: 0 bytes)
```

Benchmarking the explicit loop with and without `LoopVectorization.@turbo` strongly suggests that this is a vectorization issue:

```julia
julia> @btime let x=$x, z=$z
           for i in eachindex(x)
               z[i] = f1(x[i])
           end
       end
  2.177 μs (0 allocations: 0 bytes)

julia> using LoopVectorization

julia> @btime let x=$x, z=$z
           @turbo for i in eachindex(x)
               z[i] = f1(x[i])
           end
       end
  625.839 ns (0 allocations: 0 bytes)
```

In other words, the two-source version of `map!` is able to vectorize this loop while the single-source version is not.

The equivalent definition `f(x) = mapreduce(f, max, x)` does not have this problem. `minimum` is the same.

```julia
julia> x = [[1, 2], [3, 4]];

julia> f(x) = maximum(f, x); f(x::Number) = x;

julia> @code_warntype f(x)
MethodInstance for f(::Vector{Vector{Int64}})
  from f(x) in Main at REPL[19]:1
Arguments
  #self#::Core.Const(f)
  x::Vector{Vector{Int64}}
Body::Any
1 ─ %1 = Main.maximum(Main.f, x)::Any
└──      return %1


julia> g(x) = mapreduce(g, max, x); g(x::Number) = x;

julia> @code_warntype g(x)
MethodInstance for g(::Vector{Vector{Int64}})
  from g(x) in Main at REPL[21]:1
Arguments
  #self#::Core.Const(g)
  x::Vector{Vector{Int64}}
Body::Int64
1 ─ %1 = Main.mapreduce(Main.g, Main.max, x)::Int64
└──      return %1
```

Stumbled upon this in #44893. Perhaps related to #43461, but not the same as this is still a problem after #43467, and `maximum(f, x[1])` infers.

I'm using version 1.7.2. @MasonProtter confirmed this is still an issue on master.
