{"action":"created","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/OrdinaryDiffEq.jl/issues/1510","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/OrdinaryDiffEq.jl","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/OrdinaryDiffEq.jl/issues/1510/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/OrdinaryDiffEq.jl/issues/1510/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/OrdinaryDiffEq.jl/issues/1510/events","html_url":"https://github.com/SciML/OrdinaryDiffEq.jl/issues/1510","id":1034034209,"node_id":"I_kwDOBEVPe849ohwh","number":1510,"title":"Error when using custom Wfact or Wfact_t in SplitODEProblem","user":{"login":"dennisYatunin","id":8855258,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjg4NTUyNTg=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/8855258?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/dennisYatunin","html_url":"https://github.com/dennisYatunin","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/dennisYatunin/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/dennisYatunin/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/dennisYatunin/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/dennisYatunin/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/dennisYatunin/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/dennisYatunin/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/dennisYatunin/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/dennisYatunin/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/dennisYatunin/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":2,"created_at":"2021-10-23T01:07:10Z","updated_at":"2022-04-09T08:27:02Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"In order to use a custom `Wfact` or `Wfact_t` with a split ODE solver, one needs to specify a problem of the form `SplitODEProblem(ODEFunction(f_implicit!; Wfact_t = custom_wfact_t!), f_explicit!, u0, tspan, p)`. [This results](https://github.com/SciML/SciMLBase.jl/blob/7f81097a492ab1707209530627a494bfb10c90c5/src/problems/ode_problems.jl#L186-L228) in an `ODEProblem` that contains a `SplitFunction` `f`, where the specified custom value of `Wfact_t` (or `Wfact`) is stored in `f.f1.Wfact_t`. The corresponding field in `f`, `f.Wfact_t`, contains its [default value](https://github.com/SciML/SciMLBase.jl/blob/7f81097a492ab1707209530627a494bfb10c90c5/src/scimlfunctions.jl#L533) of `nothing`.

In order to determine whether a custom `Wfact_t` has been provided, [`has_Wfact_t(f)`](https://github.com/SciML/SciMLBase.jl/blob/7f81097a492ab1707209530627a494bfb10c90c5/src/scimlfunctions.jl#L1324) checks `f.f1.Wfact_t`, rather than `f.Wfact_t`. When computing the value of `W`, `calc_W!` [checks if `has_Wfact_t(f)` when the solver requires `Wfact_t`](https://github.com/SciML/OrdinaryDiffEq.jl/blob/3a9b6dc35e3ed19ddda4801cc5c791bf8771f956/src/derivative_utils.jl#L468) (or it [checks if  `has_Wfact(f)` when the solver requires `Wfact`](https://github.com/SciML/OrdinaryDiffEq.jl/blob/3a9b6dc35e3ed19ddda4801cc5c791bf8771f956/src/derivative_utils.jl#L473)). If the problem is specified as above, this condition is satisfied. Unfortunately, on the following line, `calc_W!` calls [`f.Wfact_t(W, u, p, dtgamma, t)`](https://github.com/SciML/OrdinaryDiffEq.jl/blob/3a9b6dc35e3ed19ddda4801cc5c791bf8771f956/src/derivative_utils.jl#L469) (or [`f.Wfact(W, u, p, dtgamma, t)`](https://github.com/SciML/OrdinaryDiffEq.jl/blob/3a9b6dc35e3ed19ddda4801cc5c791bf8771f956/src/derivative_utils.jl#L474)). Since `f.Wfact_t` is `nothing` for a `SplitFunction`, this causes an error.

There is also another issue when the problem is specified as `SplitODEProblem(ODEFunction(f_implicit!; jac_prototype = CustomWRepresentation(), Wfact_t = custom_wfact_t!), f_explicit!, u0, tspan, p)` in order to make use of a problem-specific struct for representing `W`. Just as with `Wfact` and `Wfact_t`, the custom struct is stored in `f.f1.jac_prototype`, while `f.jac_prototype` contains its [default value](https://github.com/SciML/SciMLBase.jl/blob/7f81097a492ab1707209530627a494bfb10c90c5/src/scimlfunctions.jl#L530) of `nothing`. When the algorithm cache is being built, `build_J_W` only uses a custom struct for representing `W` if [`f.jac_prototype !== nothing`](https://github.com/SciML/OrdinaryDiffEq.jl/blob/3a9b6dc35e3ed19ddda4801cc5c791bf8771f956/src/derivative_utils.jl#L606), so it ignores the custom `jac_prototype` when the problem is specified as above.

These issues can be fixed as follows:
```
import SciMLBase: SplitFunction
function SplitFunction{iip}(f1,f2; kwargs...) where iip
  f1 = ODEFunction(f1)
  f2 = ODEFunction{iip}(f2)
  SplitFunction{iip,RECOMPILE_BY_DEFAULT}(f1,f2; kwargs...,
                                          Wfact = f1.Wfact,
                                          Wfact_t = f1.Wfact_t,
                                          jac_prototype = f1.jac_prototype,
  )
end
```
However, this solution causes custom values of `Wfact`, `Wfact_t`, and `jac_prototype` to be stored in two places for split ODE problems (in both `f` and `f.f1`). An alternative approach could be to make getter functions for `Wfact`, `Wfact_t`, and `jac_prototype`, and to use those functions in `calc_W!` and `build_J_W` instead of directly accessing the fields of `f`.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/OrdinaryDiffEq.jl/issues/1510/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/OrdinaryDiffEq.jl/issues/1510/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/OrdinaryDiffEq.jl/issues/comments/1093805711","html_url":"https://github.com/SciML/OrdinaryDiffEq.jl/issues/1510#issuecomment-1093805711","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/OrdinaryDiffEq.jl/issues/1510","id":1093805711,"node_id":"IC_kwDOBEVPe85BMiaP","user":{"login":"danielwe","id":5809017,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjU4MDkwMTc=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/5809017?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe","html_url":"https://github.com/danielwe","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2022-04-09T08:27:02Z","updated_at":"2022-04-09T08:27:02Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"Hitting the same issue with just a basic `jac` specification for `f1!`, i.e., when using `SplitFunction(ODEFunction(f1!; jac=jac1!), f2!)`. Also noting that if I move the `jac` specification to `SplitFunction` only, i.e., `f = SplitFunction(f1!, f2!; jac=jac1!)`, the errors disappear and I get some performance benefit, however to get the full performance of custom `jac` I have to put it in both places: `SplitFunction(ODEFunction(f1!; jac=jac1!), f2!; jac=jac1!)`.

Basically, I think the nonlinear solvers and subroutines need to consistently access properties on `nlsolve_f(integrator)`, not `integrator.f`. That probably includes the checks `has_jac`, `has_Wfact`, etc. The forwarding of these calls from `f::SplitFunction` to `f.f1` is inconsistent.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/OrdinaryDiffEq.jl/issues/comments/1093805711/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

prob4 = prob3

jac!(jac, u, p, t) = fill!(jac, zero(eltype(jac)))  # Correct jacobian for the whole function

{"action":"created","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/44893","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/44893/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/44893/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/44893/events","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/44893","id":1195664003,"node_id":"PR_kwDOABkWpM41ybzH","number":44893,"title":"Make iteration","user":{"login":"danielwe","id":5809017,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjU4MDkwMTc=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/5809017?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe","html_url":"https://github.com/danielwe","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[{"id":62693664,"node_id":"MDU6TGFiZWw2MjY5MzY2NA==","url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/labels/arrays","name":"arrays","color":"006b75","default":false,"description":"[a, functions"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":1,"created_at":"2022-04-07T08:00:53Z","updated_at":"2022-04-09T19:30:27Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"draft":false,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/pulls/44893","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/44893","diff_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/44893.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/44893.patch","merged_at":null},"body":"Hi all, I was going to raise an issue but figured I might as well frame it in terms of a proposed solution.

I stumbled upon a case where I couldn't `isapprox` two GPU arrays because they hit this `zip` trying to pull elements one by one. Using `mapreduce` instead should fix the problem for these and any other array types that don't like sequential iteration.

The drawback is that this hurts performance for regular CPU arrays (#38558). The slowdown is worse in cases that admit an early short-circuit:

```julia
julia> A = randn(100000); B = copy(A); A[end] = Inf;  # No short-circuit

julia> @btime isapprox(A, B);
  172.186 μs (2 allocations: 781.30 KiB)

julia> @btime isapprox_new(A, B)
  292.771 μs (12 allocations: 879.28 KiB)

julia> A[1] = 0;  # Immediate short-circuit possible

julia> @btime isapprox(A, B);
  79.641 μs (2 allocations: 781.30 KiB)

julia> @btime isapprox_new(A, B);
  292.528 μs (12 allocations: 879.28 KiB)
```

However, note that:

* This branch is a fallback for an exceptional case
* Much worse performance can occur for unrelated reasons, for example:

```julia
julia> A[1] = Inf;  # Why does this make norm(A - B) an order of magnitude slower??

julia> @btime isapprox(A, B);
  726.410 μs (2 allocations: 781.30 KiB)

julia> @btime isapprox_new(A, B);
  966.347 μs (12 allocations: 879.28 KiB)
```

functions"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":4,"created_at":"2022-04-07T08:00:53Z","updated_at":"2022-04-11T00:54:01Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"draft":false,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/pulls/44893","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/44893","diff_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/44893.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/44893.patch","merged_at":null},"body":"Hi all, I was going to raise an issue but figured I might as well frame it in terms of a proposed solution.

I stumbled upon a case where I couldn't `isapprox` two GPU arrays because they hit this `zip` trying to pull elements one by one. Using `mapreduce` instead should fix the problem for these and any other array types that don't like sequential iteration.

The drawback is that this hurts performance for regular CPU arrays (#38558). The slowdown is worse in cases that admit an early short-circuit:

```julia
julia> A = randn(100000); B = copy(A); A[end] = Inf;  # No short-circuit

julia> @btime isapprox(A, B);
  172.186 μs (2 allocations: 781.30 KiB)

julia> @btime isapprox_new(A, B)
  292.771 μs (12 allocations: 879.28 KiB)

julia> A[1] = 0;  # Immediate short-circuit possible

julia> @btime isapprox(A, B);
  79.641 μs (2 allocations: 781.30 KiB)

julia> @btime isapprox_new(A, B);
  292.528 μs (12 allocations: 879.28 KiB)
```

However, note that:

* This branch is a fallback for an exceptional case
* Much worse performance can occur for unrelated reasons, for example:

```julia
julia> A[1] = Inf;  # Why does this make norm(A - B) an order of magnitude slower??

julia> @btime isapprox(A, B);
  726.410 μs (2 allocations: 781.30 KiB)

julia> @btime isapprox_new(A, B);
  966.347 μs (12 allocations: 879.28 KiB)
```

So maybe a temporary slowdown (until `mapreduce` is improved) in this exceptional branch is an acceptable price to pay for more generic code? That way GPUArrays.jl won't have to duplicate the entire method just to change one line.

~Bonus: some `mapreduce` calls in `generic_norm` are really just straightforward applications of `minimum/maximum/sum`, so I rewrote them as such for improved clarity. The behavior should be unchanged. Since this change also concerns reductions in generic linear algebra routines I thought a single PR might suffice even if this is really a separate issue.~

**About tests**

* `isapprox` isn't explicitly tested on arrays. On the other hand, it's the foundation for almost every other test, so in that sense, it's very thoroughly tested.
* ~The `generic_norm` rewrites shouldn't change behavior, so existing tests should suffice.~

EDIT: rolled back the mapreduce simplifications in `norm` since they trigger #44906 and are unrelated to the main issue here.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/44893/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/44893/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1094433586","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/44893#issuecomment-1094433586","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/44893","id":1094433586,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM5BO7sy","user":{"login":"danielwe","id":5809017,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjU4MDkwMTc=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/5809017?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe","html_url":"https://github.com/danielwe","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2022-04-11T00:54:01Z","updated_at":"2022-04-11T00:54:01Z","author_association":"NONE","body":"> > Why does this make norm(A - B) an order of magnitude slower??

call"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":15,"created_at":"2022-03-01T13:43:52Z","updated_at":"2022-03-07T07:34:02Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"Currently, Julia has pretty good support for `Tuple` restructuring: in function arguments, and assignments.

```julia
f((a, b), c) = a*b + c

h = (1, 2)
(a, b) = h
```

It would be quite nice if `Pair`s could be similarly destructured, i.e.
```julia
f(a => b, c) = a*b + c

h = 1 => 2
a => b = h
```

I find the benefit particularly apparent in function calls. For example, should one want to create an inverse function to `countmap` (from `StatsBase`):
```julia
mapcount(counts::Dict{<:Any, Int}) =
  Iterators.flatten(counts |> collect .|> (val => freq) -> repeat([val], freq)) |> collect
```

Instead, one currently has to use the following syntax:
```julia
mapcount(counts::Dict{<:Any, Int}) =
  Iterators.flatten(counts |> collect .|> valfreq -> repeat([first(valfreq)], last(valfreq))) |> collect
```

I consider `valfreq -> repeat([first(valfreq)], last(valfreq)))` materially worse than `(val => freq) -> repeat([val], freq)`. It's simply less clear.

Beyond the convenience of this, since Pairs are essentially syntax sugar over 2-tuples and tuple destructuring already works, I think this makes a lot of sense in that respect also.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/44392/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/44392/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1060273914","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/44392#issuecomment-1060273914","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/44392","id":1060273914,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM4_Mn76","user":{"login":"danielwe","id":5809017,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjU4MDkwMTc=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/5809017?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe","html_url":"https://github.com/danielwe","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2022-03-07T07:34:02Z","updated_at":"2022-03-07T07:34:02Z","author_association":"NONE","body":"+1 for type declarations inside argument destructuring. I don't think it should imply that the iterable must be a tuple, though. Argument destructuring appears to me to be equivalent to splatting, so I'd expect the same behavior in the following two cases:

```julia
f(x::Foo, x) = [...]
f(iterable...)

g((x::Foo, y)) = [...]
g(iterable)
```

Maybe this deserves a separate issue after all?","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1060273914/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

Specifically, this line is the culprit: https://github.com/SciML/OrdinaryDiffEq.jl/blob/4adacd7958fdc4e0d13a2ba6823f04a8b89f95d6/src/perform_step/low_order_rk_perform_step.jl#L821

MWE, which also confirms that other autoswitching algorithms don't have this problem:

```julia
using OrdinaryDiffEq, CUDA, Printf

CUDA.allowscalar(false)

problem = ODEProblem((du, u, p, t) -> (du .= -u), cu([1f0]), 20)
stiffalg = Rosenbrock23()
for alg in (AutoDP5, AutoTsit5, AutoVern6, AutoVern7, AutoVern8, AutoVern9)
    @printf("%-9s: ", alg)
    try
        solve(problem, alg(stiffalg))
        println("Success")
    catch e
        println("ERROR: ", replace(sprint(showerror, e), r"\n.*" => ""))
    end
end
```

Output:
```
AutoDP5  : ERROR: Scalar indexing is disallowed.
AutoTsit5: Success
AutoVern6: Success
AutoVern7: Success
AutoVern8: Success
AutoVern9: Success
```

<details>
  <summary>Full stacktrace:</summary>

```
Scalar indexing is disallowed.
Invocation of getindex resulted in scalar indexing of a GPU array.
This is typically caused by calling an iterating implementation of a method.
Such implementations *do not* execute on the GPU, but very slowly on the CPU,
and therefore are only permitted from the REPL for prototyping purposes.
If you did intend to index this array, annotate the caller with @allowscalar.

```julia
alg = (integrator.alg isa CompositeAlgorithm) ? integrator.alg.algs[current] : integrator.alg
```

in each of the affected functions, and then proceed using `alg`. seems like this line in `dolinsolve` assumes that implicit => stiff, i.e., it assumes that if you're doing a linsolve you're definitely the stiff algorithm:

https://github.com/SciML/OrdinaryDiffEq.jl/blob/2d8fd6500e1be2f0f07e6ac37152aa2f629497e6/src/misc_utils.jl#L93

From what I can tell this is correct at the moment (no non-stiff implicit methods in OrdinaryDiffEq) but doesn't seem like a robust assumption given what you said above.

In general the `is_stiff` argument to `unwrap_alg` seems redundant and requires functions like `dolinsolve` to make assumptions about their calling context, not to mention it trusts that people aren't experimenting with stuff like using a non-stiff algorithm for the stiff part of an AutoSwitch. But I suppose it's a performance optimization to avoid having to access `integrator.cache.current` for the most common cases.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/OrdinaryDiffEq.jl/issues/comments/1067572833/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

{"action":"created","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/44392","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/44392/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/44392/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/44392/events","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/44392","id":1155357419,"node_id":"I_kwDOABkWpM5E3Vrr","number":44392,"title":"Pair destructuring","user":{"login":"tecosaur","id":20903656,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjIwOTAzNjU2","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/20903656?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/tecosaur","html_url":"https://github.com/tecosaur","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/tecosaur/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/tecosaur/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/tecosaur/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/tecosaur/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/tecosaur/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/tecosaur/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/tecosaur/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/tecosaur/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/tecosaur/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[{"id":115619,"node_id":"MDU6TGFiZWwxMTU2MTk=","url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/labels/doc","name":"doc","color":"02d7e1","default":false,"description":"This call"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":20,"created_at":"2022-03-01T13:43:52Z","updated_at":"2022-03-17T20:29:27Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"Currently, Julia has pretty good support for `Tuple` restructuring: in function arguments, and assignments.

```julia
f((a, b), c) = a*b + c

h = (1, 2)
(a, b) = h
```

It would be quite nice if `Pair`s could be similarly destructured, i.e.
```julia
f(a => b, c) = a*b + c

h = 1 => 2
a => b = h
```

I find the benefit particularly apparent in function calls. For example, should one want to create an inverse function to `countmap` (from `StatsBase`):
```julia
mapcount(counts::Dict{<:Any, Int}) =
  Iterators.flatten(counts |> collect .|> (val => freq) -> repeat([val], freq)) |> collect
```

Instead, one currently has to use the following syntax:
```julia
mapcount(counts::Dict{<:Any, Int}) =
  Iterators.flatten(counts |> collect .|> valfreq -> repeat([first(valfreq)], last(valfreq))) |> collect
```

I consider `valfreq -> repeat([first(valfreq)], last(valfreq)))` materially worse than `(val => freq) -> repeat([val], freq)`. It's simply less clear.

Beyond the convenience of this, since Pairs are essentially syntax sugar over 2-tuples and tuple destructuring already works, I think this makes a lot of sense in that respect also.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/44392/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/44392/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1071387355","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/44392#issuecomment-1071387355","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/44392","id":1071387355,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM4_3BLb","user":{"login":"danielwe","id":5809017,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjU4MDkwMTc=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/5809017?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe","html_url":"https://github.com/danielwe","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2022-03-17T20:29:27Z","updated_at":"2022-03-17T20:29:27Z","author_association":"NONE","body":"> The main problem is that `f((a, b))` is different from `f((a::Any, b::Any))`, which is a bit odd but maybe not the end of the world.

My humble suggestion would be to instead update the docs around argument destructuring to include the suggestion that if you find yourself wanting to do

```
f((a::Foo,))
```

the solution is to use a function barrier

```
f((a,)) = _f(a::Foo)
```

This accomplishes the objective without syntax inconsistencies and surprises.

(I guess the pair destructuring case is different. If `f(a => b)` already maps to `f((a, b)::(=>))` (which I have no opinion about) then there's no harm or surprise in having `f(a::Int => b::String)` map to `f((a, b)::(=>){Int, String})` since the iterable type is already restricted.)","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1071387355/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

The ROCK4 function count fix is solid and tested.

I don't know how I would write a test for the KuttaPRK2p5 fsallast assignment, and as a matter of fact I don't understand 100 % of what's going on with the fsal stuff in there anyway. Seems like something more should have happened to fsalfirst, and even more seems to be missing in the in-place version further down in the file. But the issue that's fixed in this PR, regarding the assignment index, seems unambiguous.

I'm not equally confident in all of the changes so feel free to pick and choose commits. There are also potential issues that I wasn't certain enough to do something about at all, see below. Feedback appreciated.

**Summary**

* Added missing `unwrap_alg` calls to make all RKC methods, including ROCK4, work in composite algorithms (`is_stiff = true`)
* Same for `QNDF` (`is_stiff = true`)
* Same for all extrapolation methods (`is_stiff = false` for explicit methods, `is_stiff = true` for implicit methods including the undocumented `ImplicitEulerBarycentricExtrapolation`)
* Same for Anas5, KuttaPRK2p5, and PDIRK44 (`is_stiff = false` for Anas5 and KuttaPRK2p5, `is_stiff = true` for PDIRK44)
* Changed `is_stiff` from `true` to `false` for FRK65 and PFRK87 (both are listed as non-stiff in the docs)
* Removed a bunch of redundant unpackings of `alg`, mostly from Lie group and DAE methods but also a couple from RKC methods

Here are the remaining methods that still reference `integrator.alg` directly and are thus incompatible with any form of `CompositeAlgorithm`:

* FunctionMap
* ExplicitRK
* McAte42 (will fail silently when part of a CompositeAlgorithm---it's just a branch adding extra steps `if typeof(integrator.alg) <: McAte42`)
* CNAB2, CNLF2, and SBDF
* Every Krylov-enabled Lie group solver _except_ LinearExponential
  * Full list: MagnusMidpoint, LieRK4, RKMK4, RKMK2, MagnusNC8, MagnusGL4, MagnusGL8, MagnusNC6, MagnusGL6, MagnusGauss4, LieEuler, MagnusLeapfrog 

Things that kinda seem wrong to me but I wasn't sure:

* LinearExponential calls `unwrap_alg(integrator, true)`, i.e., it claims to be a stiff algorithm. This is the exact solver for LTI systems. Does this make sense at all? 

**Questions/TL;DR**

* Was I correct assuming that it's a bug for FRK65 and PFRK87 to identify as stiff?
* Do any of the following have good choices for `is_stiff` or should they remain uncomposable?
  * FunctionMap (wouldn't ever make sense to compose would it?)
  * ExplicitRK (general, takes tableau, so probably can't make such an assumption?)
  * McAte42 (symplectic, perhaps that's too specialized for composition anyway, but also I'm guessing `false`?)
  * CNAB2, CNLF2, SBDF (split solvers, perhaps don't make much sense to compose but also probably `true`?)
  * Lie group solvers
* Should LinearExponential really identify as stiff?
* Would it make sense to do `unwrap_alg(integrator, nothing)` for algorithms that aren't unambiguously stiff or non-stiff (ExplicitRK, perhaps Lie group solvers?)? That way they could still participate in custom CompositeAlgorithms, just not the built-in stiffness-based autoswitching, and one could make `unwrap_alg` throw a friendly, descriptive error about the latter.

Apologies for handing out lots of work with all the recent PRs---please take it as a sign of appreciation of this took a comprehensive approach to fixing #1618. I'm not equally confident in all of the changes so feel free to pick and choose commits. There are also potential issues that I wasn't certain enough to do something about at all, see below. Feedback appreciated.

**Summary**

* Added missing `unwrap_alg` calls to make all RKC methods, including ROCK4, work in composite algorithms (`is_stiff = true`)
* Same for `QNDF` (`is_stiff = true`)
* Same for all extrapolation methods (`is_stiff = false` for explicit methods, `is_stiff = true` for implicit methods including the undocumented `ImplicitEulerBarycentricExtrapolation`)
* Same for Anas5, KuttaPRK2p5, and PDIRK44 (`is_stiff = false` for Anas5 and KuttaPRK2p5, `is_stiff = true` for PDIRK44)
* Changed `is_stiff` from `true` to `false` for FRK65 and PFRK87 (both are listed as non-stiff in the docs)
* Removed a bunch of redundant unpackings of `alg`, mostly from Lie group and DAE methods but also a couple from RKC methods

Here are the remaining methods that still reference `integrator.alg` directly and are thus incompatible with any form of `CompositeAlgorithm`:

* FunctionMap
* ExplicitRK
* McAte42 (will fail silently when part of a CompositeAlgorithm---it's just a branch adding extra steps `if typeof(integrator.alg) <: McAte42`)
* CNAB2, CNLF2, and SBDF
* Every Krylov-enabled Lie group solver _except_ LinearExponential
  * Full list: MagnusMidpoint, LieRK4, RKMK4, RKMK2, MagnusNC8, MagnusGL4, MagnusGL8, MagnusNC6, MagnusGL6, MagnusGauss4, LieEuler, MagnusLeapfrog 

Things that kinda seem wrong to me but I wasn't sure:

* LinearExponential calls `unwrap_alg(integrator, true)`, i.e., it claims to be a stiff algorithm. This is the exact solver for LTI systems. Does this make sense at all? 

**Questions/TL;DR**

* Was I correct assuming that it's a bug for FRK65 and PFRK87 to identify as stiff?
* Do any of the following have good choices for `is_stiff` or should they remain uncomposable?
  * FunctionMap (wouldn't ever make sense to compose would it?)
  * ExplicitRK (general, takes tableau, so probably can't make such an assumption?)
  * McAte42 (symplectic, perhaps that's too specialized for composition anyway, but also I'm guessing `false`?)
  * CNAB2, CNLF2, SBDF (split solvers, perhaps don't make much sense to compose but also probably `true`?)
  * Lie group solvers
* Should LinearExponential really identify as stiff?
* Would it make sense to do `unwrap_alg(integrator, nothing)` for algorithms that aren't unambiguously stiff or non-stiff (ExplicitRK, perhaps Lie group solvers?)? That way they could still participate in custom CompositeAlgorithms, just not the built-in stiffness-based autoswitching, and one could make `unwrap_alg` throw a friendly, descriptive error about the latter.

Apologies for handing out lots of work with all the recent PRs---please take it as a sign of appreciation of this package.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/OrdinaryDiffEq.jl/issues/1625/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/OrdinaryDiffEq.jl/issues/1625/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/OrdinaryDiffEq.jl/issues/comments/1073236571","html_url":"https://github.com/SciML/OrdinaryDiffEq.jl/pull/1625#issuecomment-1073236571","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/OrdinaryDiffEq.jl/issues/1625","id":1073236571,"node_id":"IC_kwDOBEVPe84_-Epb","user":{"login":"danielwe","id":5809017,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjU4MDkwMTc=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/5809017?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe","html_url":"https://github.com/danielwe","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/danielwe/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2022-03-20T12:04:24Z","updated_at":"2022-03-20T12:04:24Z","author_association":"NONE","body":"Thanks for the feedback. Changes made:

* `unwrap_alg` throws descriptive error if `integrator.alg` is an AutoSwitch and `is_stiff = nothing`
* `unwrap_alg(integrator, nothing)` now used for the remaining algorithms that need access to `alg`: FunctionMap, ExplicitRK, McAte42, SBDF, and all exponential solvers including LinearExponential
* Never mind CNAB2 and CNLF2, they didn't need `alg` and the redundant unpacking has been removed

Requesting final confirmation that `is_stiff = nothing` is the right call for symplectic integrators (McAte42) and SBDF. Wondering if one could also argue in favor of `false` and `true`, respectively.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/OrdinaryDiffEq.jl/issues/comments/1073236571/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

* Do any of the following have good choices for `is_stiff` or should they remain uncomposable?
  * FunctionMap (wouldn't ever make sense to compose would it?)
  * ExplicitRK (general, takes tableau, so probably can't make such an assumption?)
  * McAte42 (symplectic, perhaps that's too specialized for composition anyway, but also I'm guessing `false`?)
  * CNAB2, CNLF2, SBDF (split solvers, perhaps don't make much sense to compose but also probably `true`?)
  * Lie group solvers
* Should LinearExponential really identify as stiff?
* Would it make sense to do `unwrap_alg(integrator, nothing)` for algorithms that aren't unambiguously stiff or non-stiff (ExplicitRK, perhaps Lie group solvers?)? That way they could still participate in custom CompositeAlgorithms, just not the built-in stiffness-based autoswitching, and one could make `unwrap_alg` throw a friendly, descriptive error about the latter.
